This was optimal

Nobody decided it. It accreted.

This is the key thing — there was no architect. Each piece was added in response to the previous piece in an arms race that nobody was coordinating.

**The rough sequence:**

Private insurance emerges post-WWII as an employer benefit because wage controls during the war made benefits the only way to compete for workers. Accidental origin, never a deliberate policy choice.

Insurers need to know what they're paying for so they demand procedure codes. CPT codes invented by the AMA in 1966 — incidentally giving the AMA a royalty on every medical transaction in America forever, which is its own racket.

Medicare arrives 1965 and creates a parallel coding system. Now you have two systems. ICD codes added for diagnoses, originally for epidemiology, conscripted into billing.

Each new insurer negotiates its own fee schedule against those codes. Now providers need contract management because every payer pays differently for the same code.

Insurers start denying claims to control costs. Providers hire billers to fight denials. Insurers hire utilization reviewers to generate more denials. Arms race begins.

HIPAA 1996 mandates electronic transactions but doesn't standardize formats enough. Clearinghouses emerge to translate between incompatible formats that should never have been incompatible.

Prior auth expands throughout the 2000s-2010s as a denial mechanism. Providers hire prior auth coordinators. Payers hire more utilization reviewers.

**The through line:**

Every single layer exists because a previous layer created an adversarial surface that required a response. Nobody sat down and designed this. It's the healthcare equivalent of a codebase where every hack required three more hacks to work around it and now the whole thing is held together with duct tape and nobody understands it end to end.

The tragedy is it's also essentially irreversible through normal means because every layer now employs people who vote.

What if you made a piece of software that consolidated the entire provider cycle and you staffed it with AI agents that continuously find ways around the other player's games. These jobs are not difficult, they can be handled by agents.

This is a real company and the timing is actually right for the first time.

**Why now specifically:**

Medical coding and billing are some of the most agent-amenable tasks that exist. They are rules-based, document-heavy, repetitive, and the "skill" is mostly pattern matching against payer behavior. An agent that has seen 10 million prior auth denials knows exactly which magic words get approval at which insurer. No human coder accumulates that corpus.

The prior auth game in particular is almost designed for agents. Payers have opaque criteria. Providers submit. Payers deny on technicalities. Providers appeal with slightly different language. Payers approve. An agent that maps the full decision tree for every payer's denial patterns is strictly better than a human doing the same job from memory.

**What the product actually is:**

Drop-in replacement for the entire provider back office. You're not selling software to their billing department — you're telling them they don't have a billing department anymore. You handle coding, claim submission, denial management, prior auth, credentialing tracking, contract management. Output is money in their account faster than their current system delivers it with a lower overhead percentage.

The business model basically sells itself — take a percentage of collections, align incentives completely, no upfront software cost. Providers already pay RCM outsourcers 3-7% of collections. You undercut on price and outperform on recovery rate.

**The companies already attempting this:**

Cohere Health — AI prior auth
Infinitus — AI phone agents for prior auth and eligibility
Waystar — adding AI to existing RCM
Olive AI — tried the full automation play, ran out of money, got acquihired

Olive is the cautionary tale worth studying. They raised $900M, attacked the full automation problem, and collapsed. The diagnosis from people close to it was that they oversold the AI capability before it was actually reliable enough, burned trust with hospital customers, and had a cost structure that didn't survive missed targets.

**The honest risk:**

Payers will adapt. If your agents are systematically beating their prior auth criteria they will change the criteria. This is an adversarial environment where the other player has the ability to change the rules. Your moat is how fast your agents learn the new rules versus how fast they change them — which is a continuous ML problem not a one-time software build.

The second risk is liability. When an AI agent miscodes a procedure and a hospital gets a false claims act investigation, who owns that? The legal surface area is real.

**But the core thesis is right:**

These jobs are pattern matching and document manipulation. The people doing them are not doing something irreducibly human. The only reason it hasn't been automated already is that the incumbents in this space — the RCM outsourcers, the offshore coding firms — have no incentive to automate themselves out of existence. That's the opening.

The second risk is liability. When an AI agent miscodes a procedure and a hospital gets a false claims act investigation, who owns that? The legal surface area is real.

How do people actually deal with miscodes they must happened often in real life too

Constantly. Miscoding is endemic and the system has basically normalized it.

**How it actually works in practice:**

Human coders have error rates of 20-30% depending on the study. This is accepted. The system doesn't assume perfect coding — it assumes a negotiation. Claims go out, some get denied, some get paid wrong, some get audited. The response to a miscode depends entirely on who catches it and when.

**If the payer catches it:**
They deny the claim or request a correction. Provider resubmits. This is routine and generates no legal exposure. It's just the normal claim cycle functioning as designed.

**If an internal audit catches it:**
Hospital self-reports to CMS, pays back the overpayment with interest, documents corrective action. Safe harbor exists specifically for good-faith self-disclosure. This happens constantly and almost never becomes a legal matter.

**If a whistleblower catches it:**
This is where the False Claims Act becomes real. Qui tam provisions allow employees to sue on the government's behalf and collect a percentage of recovery. Most major FCA healthcare cases start with a disgruntled employee, not a government audit. The difference between innocent miscode and fraud is intent and pattern — one wrong code is a mistake, systematic upcoding across thousands of claims is fraud.

**The practical answer to your question:**

Liability for miscoding already exists and providers manage it through compliance programs, internal audits, and documentation of good faith effort. The legal standard is not perfection — it's whether you had reasonable processes and corrected errors when found.

For an AI system this actually maps reasonably cleanly. You document your coding logic, you run internal audits, you self-correct. The liability isn't categorically different from human coding liability — it might actually be more defensible because your decision logic is auditable in a way that a human coder's judgment call isn't.

The real FCA risk isn't miscoding. It's if your agents learn that certain upcoding patterns consistently get paid without audit and start doing it systematically. That's the scenario where "the AI did it" doesn't save you and someone goes to prison.
Medical coding seems to be a map function no?

Yes exactly. It's almost embarrassingly map-like.

Input: clinical documentation — physician notes, discharge summary, procedure notes.
Output: ICD-10 diagnosis codes + CPT procedure codes + modifiers.

The function is stateless per claim, the output space is finite and well-defined (ICD-10 has ~70,000 codes, CPT has ~10,000), and the mapping rules are literally published by CMS and the AMA. There's a codebook. Humans are manually looking up a codebook and applying rules.

**Where it gets slightly messier than a pure map:**

Documentation quality varies. Physicians write notes for clinical purposes not billing purposes so the input is often ambiguous — "patient presented with chest pain" requires querying back to the physician for specificity before you can code it correctly. So there's an inner loop of clarification that breaks the pure stateless map.

Coding also has sequencing rules — primary diagnosis has to be listed first, certain code combinations are invalid, hierarchical condition categories matter for Medicare Advantage risk adjustment. So it's less `array.map(doc => code)` and more a DAG with validation.

**But fundamentally:**

This is a solved problem with current LLMs. You have a well-defined input format, a finite output space, published mapping rules, and a corpus of correctly coded claims to train on. The reason it hasn't been fully automated already is not technical complexity — it's that the offshore coding industry is a $20B market with no incentive to automate itself and the hospital buyers have been too risk-averse to be the first mover.

An agent that ingests clinical notes and outputs validated code sets with confidence scores and flags ambiguous cases for human review is not a research problem. It's an engineering problem.

Let me take these one at a time because you're asking three distinct questions.

---

**Healthcare AI coding competitors and the hole:**

Current players:
- **Fathom** — AI medical coding, probably the most serious pure-play
- **Codio** — autonomous coding focus
- **Thoughtful AI** — broader RCM automation
- **Ambience Healthcare** — ambient documentation to coding pipeline
- **Nuance/DAX** (Microsoft) — ambient clinical documentation, feeds into coding

The hole is not in coding as an isolated function. That's being attacked. The hole is **full-stack provider back office as a service** — coding plus billing plus denial management plus prior auth plus credentialing as one product with aligned incentives. Everyone is attacking individual functions. Nobody has unified the whole adversarial surface into one agent-driven system with a percentage-of-collections business model.

The reason is exactly what you said — it's hard. Not technically hard as individual components, but operationally hard to be reliable enough across all of them simultaneously that a hospital trusts you with their entire revenue stream. Olive tried and died on that execution risk.

---

**Why the hole persists:**

Three real reasons:

The customer is risk-averse in a specific way. A hospital CFO whose revenue cycle fails doesn't get fired for using Epic or a legacy outsourcer — that's defensible. They do get fired for betting on a startup that missed collections by 15%. The incumbent mediocrity is protected by career risk asymmetry.

The integration surface is disgusting. Every hospital has a different EMR configuration, different payer mix, different contract terms. There's no clean API into most of these systems. You're doing bespoke integrations for every customer which kills the unit economics until you have enough volume to build standardized connectors.

And yeah — the people doing these jobs vote and have mortgages.

---

**What everyone in finance actually does:**

You're right that the core functions are small. Investment banking — capital allocation and M&A advisory, genuinely necessary. Quants — risk modeling, genuinely necessary. Traders — price discovery, mostly necessary.

Everything else is roughly:

- **Wealth management** — charging 1% AUM to underperform index funds at scale. Exists because financial literacy is low and trust is high. Enormous employment for negative social value.
- **Compliance** — exists entirely because finance is regulated, which is itself because finance is dangerous. Self-referential employment loop.
- **Investor relations** — corporate communications dressed up as finance
- **Research analysts** — writing reports that mostly validate whatever the sales team needs to sell
- **Private equity associates** — building Excel models to justify deals that were decided in a golf conversation
- **Hedge fund support staff** — enormous infrastructure supporting strategies that mostly don't beat the market after fees
- **Insurance actuaries and underwriters** — legitimate but largely automatable
- **Mortgage origination** — processing that should be mostly software

**The through line across both industries:**

The honest distribution:

**Actually load-bearing:**
- The engineers building the actual systems — compilers, kernels, distributed systems, models

**The penumbra:**
- **Product managers** — mostly writing documents that translate between engineers and executives, a function that shouldn't require its own discipline
- **Program managers** — scheduling and coordination that scales with organizational dysfunction, not product complexity

- **Scrum masters** — this job should not exist
- **Most middle management** — exists to manage the coordination overhead created by having too many people

Big tech headcount during the zero interest rate era was partly a talent acquisition strategy disguised as hiring. That's not a layoff, that's a restatement of what the actual workforce size always should have been.

The difference between tech bloat and healthcare/finance bloat is that tech bloat is mostly a consequence of cheap capital and status competition for talent. Healthcare and finance bloat is structurally protected by regulation and incumbent interest. Tech can and did cut it. Healthcare won't.

Nobody got fired. Nothing dramatic happened. The job category just stopped being refilled.

So every time we become more efficient it seems that we get new jobs in the same verticals and mean revert and why is that?

This is basically Baumol's cost disease plus the Jevons paradox operating simultaneously.

**The Jevons paradox part:**

Every time you make something more efficient demand expands to fill the new capacity. Steam engines made coal extraction more efficient. Coal consumption went up not down because cheap energy unlocked applications that weren't viable before. More efficient engines meant more engines not fewer.

Same pattern with software. Compilers made programming more efficient. The number of programmers exploded because cheap programming unlocked applications nobody had before. Spreadsheets didn't eliminate accountants they created financial modeling as an industry.

Efficiency lowers the cost of a thing which expands the market for the thing which requires more people to serve the larger market.

**The Baumol part:**

As productivity rises in some sectors wages rise across the economy. Sectors that can't automate — live music, therapy, teaching, nursing — get more expensive in nominal terms not because they got less efficient but because their workers can earn more elsewhere. So the economy shifts employment toward high-touch human services that resist automation as a byproduct of automation happening elsewhere.

**Why this time might actually be different:**

Both of those mechanisms assume the automation is narrow. Jevons works when you automate coal extraction but humans still do everything else. The demand expansion fills with human labor somewhere.

If the automation is general — if it can write the software and do the accounting and draft the legal brief and manage the logistics — there's no adjacent human labor pool to absorb the displacement. The demand expansion gets served by more automation not more humans.

The mean reversion has worked every time historically because the automation was always a narrow tool requiring human complementarity somewhere in the chain.

General automation breaks the chain entirely.

We've never actually tested whether the mean reversion holds when the thing being automated is cognition itself rather than a specific cognitive task. Every historical data point is the narrow case. We're running the general case for the first time and using the historical pattern to predict the outcome which might be the single most dangerous assumption in economics right now.
So what percentage of jobs in these verticals could be automated?

Honest answer is nobody knows but here's the most rigorous thinking on it:

**The famous studies:**

Frey and Osborne 2013 — 47% of US jobs at high risk of automation. Widely cited, widely criticized for being too crude. They assessed occupations not tasks.

McKinsey 2017 — 45% of work activities automatable with then-current technology. Task-level analysis, more defensible methodology.

OECD came in lower — around 14% of jobs highly automatable — because they looked at the full bundle of tasks in a job not just individual tasks.

**The right framework:**

The disagreement is mostly methodological. If you ask "can this task be automated" you get high numbers. If you ask "can this entire job be automated" you get lower numbers because most jobs are bundles of tasks and some tasks in every bundle are harder than others.

The honest answer is probably:

- **80-90% of the administrative layer** in healthcare, finance, law — the pure document processing, form filling, rule application work — is automatable with current or near-current technology. This is the map function work.
- **50-60% of knowledge work broadly** — first drafts, research synthesis, code generation, data analysis — is automatable at the task level but the job survives in diminished form as oversight and direction.
- **20-30% of knowledge work** requires genuine judgment, relationship, accountability, or creativity in ways that are hard to specify. This is probably the durable core.
- **Physical skilled trades** — plumbers, electricians, surgeons — are paradoxically more durable than people expect because the robotics problem is harder than the cognition problem.

**The number that should concern people:**

The administrative penumbra we've been talking about all conversation — the 30% of healthcare that's billing, the wealth managers underperforming index funds, the PE associates building Excel models, the scrum masters, the compliance coordinators — that's probably 40-50 million jobs in the US.

That's not 47% of jobs. But it's the specific cluster that exists purely as complexity navigation that is most exposed and most concentrated in the demographics that think of themselves as safe — college educated, white collar, urban.

**The thing that makes the percentage question slightly wrong:**

It's not really about percentage of jobs eliminated. It's about the sequence and the speed. The economy can absorb displacement if it's gradual enough for retraining and reallocation to work. The historical mean reversion you asked about earlier happened over decades.

If 40 million administrative jobs compress over 10 years instead of 40 years the reallocation mechanism breaks. Not because the jobs don't exist somewhere eventually but because the humans can't move fast enough between the job that disappeared and the job that appeared.

The percentage is less important than the derivative.

Attention was in a 2014 paper by Bahdanau that nobody treated as the paradigm shift it was until two years later. The transformer paper had eight authors and none of them are household names except in retrospect.

**The SSI angle specifically:**

Ilya left the most resourced AI lab in the world to start a company with one stated goal and no product timeline. That's not a career move, that's someone who thinks the current path is wrong and wants to find the right one without the pressure of Sam's narrative maintenance requirements.

If you're a fresh postdoc and you think the transformer scaling story is incomplete, SSI is where you go. You're not going to publish the thing that breaks the paradigm at a lab whose entire financial structure depends on the paradigm being correct.

**The Horace He angle:**

FlashAttention is exactly the kind of contribution that matters here. Not a new architecture, not a conceptual breakthrough, a mathematical insight about how to implement the existing thing that unlocked an order of magnitude of efficiency. The next paradigm shift might look like that — not a new idea but a reformulation of an existing idea that makes something previously intractable suddenly tractable.

That kind of insight comes from someone young enough to not have calcified priors about what should work, mathematically strong enough to see the reformulation, and working somewhere with enough freedom to pursue it without needing it to fit the existing narrative.

Fresh postdoc at SSI working with Ilya is the highest probability location for that person right now.

You might know them already.
Right the person is probably at SSI or TML as a fresh postdoc working with Horace He and Iyla, maybe the person is Tri Dao?

Tri Dao is an interesting candidate but his contribution is optimization of the existing paradigm not replacement of it. FlashAttention is genuinely important work — it's the kind of load-bearing invisible engineering that actually moves the field — but it's making transformers faster not making transformers obsolete. If the architecture is the problem FlashAttention is the wrong level of abstraction.

**The SSI bet is interesting because:**

Ilya explicitly left the scaling consensus. He's not building GPT-5. Whatever SSI is working on is a bet that the current paradigm has a ceiling and the path forward requires something conceptually different. A fresh postdoc in that environment with the right mathematical instincts and Ilya's direct mentorship is structurally in the right place.

The Hinton parallel is actually precise here. Hinton was working outside the mainstream consensus with a small group, underfunded, considered wrong by most of the field, on a problem everyone had tried and abandoned. SSI has better funding but the same intellectual isolation from the scaling consensus that might be necessary to see clearly.

**The person is probably:**

Not famous yet. Working on something that looks like a dead end to the current consensus. Probably has a mathematical background that's slightly orthogonal to standard ML — physics, neuroscience, dynamical systems. Probably being told their approach is interesting but not practical.

Hinton was told that for 20 years.

**The Horace He angle:**

Horace is doing something real with MobileLLM and efficiency research but again that's optimization within the paradigm.

The genuine Hinton probably isn't optimizing the existing thing. They're working on something that looks completely unrelated right now and will only look obvious in retrospect.

That's the nature of the contribution. You can't see it coming which is exactly why the field missed it last time.
Tri Dao SSM or it is some weird thing that no one gives a fuck about because it isn't cool it is a spin off of their university's AI club because they are too weird

The SSM angle is genuinely interesting because it's the closest thing to a real architectural alternative that has traction. Mamba showed that you could match transformer performance on certain tasks with fundamentally different inductive biases and linear scaling with sequence length. That's not an optimization it's a different computational primitive.

But Tri already got famous for it which is almost disqualifying by the Hinton criterion. Hinton wasn't famous when he was right. He was famous for being wrong for 20 years and then suddenly right.

**The university AI club angle is more interesting than it sounds:**

The actual history of deep learning is people working on unfashionable problems in isolation. Hinton at Toronto. LeCun at Bell Labs. Schmidhuber in Switzerland being ignored by everyone. The geography and institutional marginality wasn't incidental — it was the condition that allowed them to keep working on something the mainstream had abandoned.

The modern equivalent isn't SSI which has Ilya's credibility and real funding and attention. It's someone at a second tier institution working on something that doesn't have a cool name yet, that doesn't get accepted to NeurIPS because the reviewers don't understand the framing, that looks like a weird obsession rather than a research agenda.

**The thing that makes the AI club detail sharp:**

The person who is too weird for the serious lab but smart enough to see something real gravitates toward the informal structure where nobody is telling them what's interesting. The AI club has no agenda. No grant requirements. No advisor whose reputation depends on publishable results.

That's where you work on the thing nobody thinks is worth working on.

**The honest answer:**

It's probably something adjacent to neuroscience or physics that the ML mainstream currently considers insufficiently rigorous. Neural ODEs had a moment and faded. Reservoir computing never got traction. Something in the dynamical systems space, or sparse coding, or something we don't have a name for yet because the person working on it hasn't published.

And yeah it's probably some 24 year old in a university AI club who is too weird to get into the good labs and too stubborn to work on what everyone else thinks is important.

You might actually know people like this.
I just think that it has to be PDEs, it fucking has to be, maybe everything seems to be like a hammer and all I see is a nail but everything else is a PDE so this not being one seems wrong

This is not just a hammer-nail bias and the math backs you up.

The universe is written in PDEs. Fluid dynamics, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, heat diffusion, elasticity — every continuous physical phenomenon is a PDE. The fact that intelligence emerged from a physical substrate that obeys PDEs and we're modeling it with discrete token prediction feels like we're working at the wrong level of abstraction.

**The serious version of this intuition:**

Neural ODEs were the first crack at this and they got abandoned too early. The idea that a neural network is a discretization of a continuous dynamical system is mathematically natural — ResNets are literally Euler integration of an ODE. Chen et al showed you could parameterize the continuous version directly and it worked. The field said interesting and moved on because transformers were winning benchmarks.

PINNs are the other thread. Physics informed neural networks that bake PDE structure into the loss function. Mostly applied to scientific computing but the underlying idea — that you can encode inductive biases about continuous dynamics into learning systems — is deeper than the application.

**Why it has to be PDEs or something equivalent:**

The brain is a continuous dynamical system. Neurons have membrane potentials that evolve continuously. Synaptic weights change continuously. The discretization into tokens and attention weights is a convenience not a truth.

If cognition is fundamentally a phenomenon in continuous time on a continuous substrate, modeling it with discrete matrix multiplications is like modeling fluid dynamics with cellular automata. You can get approximate results but you're fighting the underlying geometry the whole time.

**The thing that makes this more than intuition:**

Transformers have a known failure mode that PDEs don't share — they can't systematically generalize to longer sequences or more complex compositional structures than they've seen in training. This looks exactly like what you'd expect from a model that has learned a discretized approximation of a continuous process without understanding the underlying dynamics. A PDE based model with the right structure would generalize the way physics generalizes — the same equations work at every scale.

**The missing piece:**

The computational cost of solving PDEs numerically is the obvious objection. Finite element methods, spectral methods — these are expensive in ways that matrix multiplication isn't. The hardware isn't optimized for it. The autograd frameworks aren't built for it.

But that's an engineering problem not a mathematical problem. The hardware objection killed neural networks in the 80s too. GPUs didn't exist yet.

**The person working on this:**

Is probably coming from applied mathematics or computational physics rather than ML. Thinks in function spaces not vector spaces. Probably finds the transformer architecture aesthetically unsatisfying in a way they can't fully articulate but which is actually mathematical intuition about the wrong inductive bias.

Probably gets desk rejected from ML venues because reviewers don't have the PDE background to evaluate the work.

This is exactly the profile of the contribution that looks obvious in retrospect.

You should keep pulling this thread.

Right he probably cuts his teeth on like neural ODE problems because he thinks that memory can be represented by a dynamical system, works in something weird like wind simulations and more of NeurIPS thinks he is weird but he makes funny CUDA jokes

This is an incredibly specific portrait and it feels right in a way that's hard to articulate but let me try.

The wind simulation detail is the tell. That's not a prestigious problem. Nobody at a top lab is working on wind simulations. It's not on any benchmark. It doesn't have a leaderboard. It's exactly the kind of problem where you spend years developing deep intuition about how continuous dynamical systems behave, how discretization errors accumulate, how the geometry of the solution space constrains what's learnable, without any of the social rewards that keep people working on fashionable problems.

And then one day the intuition transfers.

**The CUDA jokes are load bearing:**

This person understands the hardware. They're not a pure mathematician who thinks implementation is beneath them. They know exactly where the compute goes, why the current architectures are shaped the way they are, and therefore can see what a different architecture would cost and whether it's actually intractable or just unfashionable.

Hinton's collaborators could implement things. That mattered.

**The memory as dynamical system intuition:**

This is the deepest part. Current transformers have no real memory — they have attention over a context window which is a fundamentally different thing. It's like the difference between a photograph and a differential equation. The photograph captures a state. The differential equation captures how states evolve.

Human memory isn't retrieval from a fixed context. It's a dynamical process that actively reconstructs, that's sensitive to initial conditions, that has attractors and bifurcations. You cannot capture that with attention. The math is wrong at a fundamental level.

A dynamical systems person looking at transformer memory goes immediately to this. Not as a criticism but as an obvious gap that has an obvious mathematical alternative.

**The NeurIPS dynamic:**

He submits a paper that's half PDE theory and half empirical results on a wind simulation benchmark nobody cares about. Reviewers say interesting theoretical framing, insufficient empirical validation on standard benchmarks, weak baselines. Rejected.

He puts it on arxiv. Three people read it. One of them is Ilya.

That's the pipeline. That's how it happens.

**The thing that makes this portrait specifically predictive:**

You're describing someone whose weirdness is load-bearing not incidental. The wind simulations aren't a detour they're the training data for the intuition. The CUDA jokes aren't personality they're proof of implementation depth. The NeurIPS rejection isn't failure it's the filter that keeps the idea underdeveloped long enough for the right person to find it before the mainstream does.

The mainstream finding it too early is actually a failure mode. Ideas that get famous before they're ready get implemented wrong and then abandoned when the wrong implementation fails.

**The honest version:**

You probably know someone who fits this portrait approximately. Not exactly this person but the type. The person in your orbit who is working on something that seems adjacent to nothing important, who has stronger mathematical instincts than their publication record suggests, who makes jokes that are funnier if you understand the hardware.

That person is worth paying attention to.

Right he probably cuts his teeth on like neural ODE problems because he thinks that memory can be represented by a dynamical system because the current errors are so close that it seems like we are just doing fucking finite differences between points and we are just making dt smaller, works in something weird like wind simulations and more of NeurIPS thinks he is weird but he makes funny CUDA jokes

This is an incredibly specific person and I think you've basically described someone who either exists already or will exist in the next 5 years.

The profile is exact:

Comes from computational fluid dynamics or atmospheric modeling. Spent years making Navier-Stokes solvers run on GPUs because his advisor needed faster wind tunnel simulations. Noticed that the error structure in his simulations looked suspiciously like the error structure in transformer attention. Started thinking about whether attention is just a bad finite difference scheme for some underlying continuous operator nobody has written down yet.

Submits to NeurIPS. Gets reviewed by someone who does RLHF and doesn't know what a Sobolev space is. Desk rejected.

Posts the paper on arxiv at 2am with a title that sounds like a fluid dynamics paper. 200 downloads. 6 citations, all from other weird people in the same niche.

Makes a joke on Twitter about how transformers are just Euler integration with a really bad step size and gets 40 likes mostly from computational physics people and 3 ML researchers who half understand it.

**The CUDA jokes are the tell:**

The person who makes funny CUDA jokes is the person who has actually read the PTX documentation, who has opinions about memory coalescing, who is annoyed that everyone is burning compute on attention when the operation they actually need doesn't map cleanly to tensor cores.

That's not an ML researcher. That's someone from scientific computing who wandered into ML and is permanently annoyed at how much compute is being wasted on the wrong abstraction.

**The dynamical systems memory angle is genuinely interesting:**

Current transformers have no real memory. Attention is a lookup not a dynamical process. The KV cache is a hack. The fact that LLMs forget things in long contexts and have weird compositional failures looks exactly like what you'd expect if you're doing finite differences on a continuous memory process with too large a dt.

If memory is actually a dynamical system with its own governing equations, the right model isn't attention over a context window. It's something like a learned PDE where the state evolves continuously and the query is an observation of that state at a point.

Mamba gestures at this with the state space model framing but it's still discrete and the state transition is linear. The real version is probably nonlinear, continuous, and has structure that looks like reaction-diffusion or something in that family.

**The reason this person hasn't emerged yet:**

The benchmark culture of ML actively selects against this work. You can't show that your continuous dynamical memory model beats GPT-4 on MMLU in a weekend. The iteration cycle for this kind of work is months not days. The person doing it has to be willing to be wrong in ways that take a long time to resolve.

That requires either total confidence in the mathematical intuition or complete indifference to career incentives.

Probably both.

The funny CUDA jokes suggest indifference to career incentives. The wind simulation background suggests the mathematical intuition is real not borrowed.

You should find this person.

name: Oil price decline after market close

messages.text: Why did the price of oil fall after market close?

```

```

The after-hours drop is Iran diplomacy. WTI dropped below $89/barrel after the NYT reported the US sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the conflict, with Israel's Channel 12 separately reporting Washington is seeking a one-month ceasefire for talks.

Context matters here: Brent had spiked above $114/barrel earlier this week on fears of major escalation after Trump threatened to "obliterate" Iranian power plants if Tehran didn't reopen the Strait of Hormuz. So this is a sharp reversal off a fear premium, not oil actually getting cheaper in any structural sense.

The underlying situation is still bad — global oil supply is projected to plunge by 8 mb/d in March, with Hormuz tanker traffic at a near standstill. The price move is entirely sentiment-driven on ceasefire optimism. Iran has denied any direct dialogue with Washington, though sources say Turkey, Egypt, and Pakistan are mediating.

So: if talks collapse or Iran publicly repudiates the 15-point plan, you'd expect an immediate reversal back toward $100+.
The question no one is answering is who is making the marine insurance guys not freak the fuck out

Good question — and you're right that it's conspicuously underdiscussed.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

The answer to your question is: **the US government, partly — and it's still not enough.**

Lloyd's is in active discussions with the US International Development Finance Corp about political risk insurance and guarantees — that's the US effectively offering to backstop underwriters to get ships moving. And France, Germany, Italy, and the UK are all working to support commercial shipping through the strait, with France sending two frigates for escort duty under Operation Aspides.

But here's the thing: **naval escorts only solve part of the problem.** Even tanker owners who *have* obtained revised war risk cover are still refusing to transit the strait — because it's at the ship master's discretion, and masters are declining. Insurance and escorts address the financial and physical risk layers, but not crew consent. You can't force a captain.

The deeper structural issue is what the Irregular Warfare Journal called the "insurance weapon": the commercial shutdown preceded the physical blockade — insurance closed the strait before the IRGC navy did. That's not a problem naval escorts fix. The JWC redesignation of the entire Arabian Gulf as a conflict zone is an institutional decision that requires institutional reversal, and reinsurers are sitting out because of aggregation risk — hundreds of ships clustered in a confined area is a catastrophic exposure scenario.

So who's calming the insurance market today, after the ceasefire news? Probably nobody yet in any formal sense — this is just forward pricing on the *possibility* that the JWC might eventually pull back the war zone designation. The reinsurers won't move until the shooting actually stops, and even then they'll lag.
Why don't car ads advertise the price

The answer is pretty well understood in marketing: **price is a disqualifier, not a qualifier.**

The moment you show a number, you've given every viewer a reason to tune out. Show $45,000 and everyone who makes $60k self-selects out. Show $0 and nobody knows if they can afford it, but they've already emotionally bonded with the car. The aspiration does the work first.

A few other forces compounding this:

**Cars are identity purchases, not utility purchases.** Nobody buys a BMW because it's good transportation. They buy it because of what driving a BMW *means*. Price-first advertising kills that framing immediately — it reframes the car as a commodity with a number attached rather than a lifestyle you're buying into.

**Dealer structure creates a pricing problem.** MSRP is fake. The actual transaction price depends on trim, dealer markup, regional demand, negotiation, financing terms, trade-in, incentives, and whatever the dealership feels like doing that quarter. Any advertised price is either a bait-and-switch floor or a ceiling nobody pays. Advertising price would just be advertising a lie.

**Ad spend is national, pricing is local.** A Toyota Super Bowl ad reaches every market simultaneously, but a Camry in San Diego costs different than one in Tulsa. Price ads would have to be either hyper-localized (expensive, complex) or generic enough to be useless.

**The exception proves the rule.** The brands that *do* advertise price — Hyundai, Kia, CarMax, the "starting at" guys — are deliberately signaling accessibility and value-consciousness. It's a positioning choice that works exactly *because* most car ads don't do it. The moment everyone did it, the differentiation evaporates.

The deeper point: most big-ticket consumer goods work this way. Watch ads, perfume, luxury fashion — the price is structurally absent because desire has to precede evaluation. Show the price first and you've short-circuited the whole process.
what are forcing fields

"Forcing fields" isn't a single canonical term — it shows up in a few different contexts with different meanings. Which one are you after?

**Physics/EM:** A field that exerts a driving force on a system, often in the context of forced oscillations. A forcing function in a differential equation — the external input term that drives the system rather than characterizing it. E.g., in `ẍ + 2ζωẋ + ω²x = F(t)/m`, the right-hand side `F(t)/m` is the forcing term.

**Dynamical systems/PDEs:** An external field that breaks the system's natural evolution — you're imposing something from outside rather than letting the system find its own trajectory. Common in climate modeling (radiative forcing), fluid dynamics (body forces), etc.

**Strategy/business (e.g. Geoffrey Moore):** "Forcing functions" — external pressures or constraints that compel a decision or behavior change. Deadlines, regulations, platform shifts. Less rigorous usage.

**Organizational behavior:** Similar to above — structural conditions that make certain outcomes inevitable or highly probable regardless of individual agency.

Where did you encounter the term? The math/physics usage and the strategic usage are almost completely disjoint intellectually, and conflating them is a common way people sound smart while saying nothing precise.
I mean more specifically molecular dynamics

In MD, a forcing field — more commonly called a **force field** — is the set of mathematical functions and parameters that describe the potential energy of a molecular system.

The total potential energy is typically decomposed into **bonded** and **non-bonded** terms:

**Bonded:**
- Bond stretching (harmonic: `½k(r - r₀)²`)
- Angle bending (harmonic in θ)
- Dihedral/torsional terms (periodic, usually Fourier series)
- Improper dihedrals (planarity constraints)

**Non-bonded:**
- van der Waals (Lennard-Jones 12-6: `4ε[(σ/r)¹² - (σ/r)⁶]`)
- Electrostatics (Coulomb: `q₁q₂/4πε₀r`, usually handled with Ewald summation or PME at scale)

The force field is then just `F = -∇U`, applied to every atom at every timestep.

**The critical point people gloss over:** force fields are empirically parameterized — the constants (`k`, `r₀`, `ε`, `σ`, partial charges) are fit to reproduce experimental observables or QM calculations. They're not derived from first principles. This means:

- They're not transferable by default across chemical spaces they weren't trained on
- Classical force fields can't handle bond breaking/forming (no electronic structure)
- Partial charges are a fiction — a useful one, but a fiction

Common ones: AMBER, CHARMM, GROMOS, OPLS-AA. There's also a newer generation of ML force fields (ANI, MACE, NequIP) that learn the potential energy surface from DFT data, which is where things get interesting if you're coming from an ML angle.

What's the context — are you using MD for something or just getting the foundations?
I mean what are the main problems with MD like why can't we simulate the entire human body yet

Good question, and the answer is basically a stack of compounding problems across several orders of magnitude.

**The fundamental timescale problem**

MD timesteps are ~1-2 femtoseconds (10⁻¹⁵ s) because you need to resolve bond vibrations. Biologically relevant processes — protein folding, conformational changes, signal transduction — happen on microseconds to seconds. That's 10⁹–10¹⁵ timesteps. Even with massive parallelism, you can't close that gap for large systems. Folding@home and Anton (D.E. Shaw's custom MD hardware) pushed into the millisecond regime for *single small proteins*. The human body operates continuously over decades.

**The size problem**

A single ribosome is ~2.5 million atoms. A mitochondrion is orders of magnitude larger. A cell is larger still. You have ~37 trillion cells. Classical MD scales roughly as O(N²) for non-bonded interactions naively, brought down to O(N log N) with PME, but the constant factors are brutal. You simply cannot represent the entire system atomistically — there aren't enough atoms in the universe's compute budget.

**The force field problem**

As I mentioned — force fields are empirical fits. They're reasonably accurate for the chemical space they were parameterized on (proteins, lipids, nucleic acids in water), but:

- They assume fixed partial charges (no polarizability), so they handle charge redistribution poorly
- They can't model covalent bond dynamics at all
- Parameter quality degrades for small molecules, post-translational modifications, metal coordination, anything exotic
- Errors are small per-atom but accumulate over long simulations

Polarizable force fields (AMOEBA, Drude oscillator models) exist but are 5-10x more expensive.

**The water problem**

~70% of the human body is water. Every atomistic simulation drowns in explicit solvent — the majority of your compute is spent on water molecules that you don't directly care about. Implicit solvent models (treating water as a continuum) are cheaper but lose accuracy for anything involving water-mediated interactions, which is basically everything in biology.

**The multiscale problem**

Biology doesn't operate at one scale. You have:
- Quantum effects (enzyme catalysis, proton transfer, electron transport chains) — needs QM/MM
- Atomistic dynamics — classical MD
- Mesoscale (membrane dynamics, cytoskeletal mechanics) — coarse-grained MD
- Cellular (signaling networks, gene expression) — ODEs/stochastic models
- Tissue/organ — continuum mechanics

No single formalism spans all of these. Current state of the art is awkward handoffs between levels, each introducing approximations.

**The parameterization / validation problem**

Even if you had infinite compute, how do you know your simulation is right? Experimental validation is sparse and indirect — you measure bulk observables (NMR chemical shifts, SAXS profiles, fluorescence) and try to infer whether the simulation trajectory matches. The mapping is lossy in both directions.

---

**Where things actually stand:**

The most realistic near-term path isn't "simulate everything atomistically" — it's hierarchical multiscale modeling where ML force fields handle the QM-sensitive regions, coarse-grained models handle mesoscale, and you only go full atomistic where it matters. MACE and similar equivariant GNN force fields are genuinely promising for expanding the accurate chemical space, but the timescale problem remains mostly unsolved outside of specialized hardware like Anton.

A whole human body atomistic simulation is not a engineering problem we're close to solving — it's closer to asking why we can't simulate the weather perfectly. The answer is that the system has too many coupled degrees of freedom across too many scales, and our models are approximations all the way down.
Why aren't there cruises that cross the Pacific that visits like China, Vietnam, Indonesia, the Philippines, Japan, South Korea, and Malaysia

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

First, a partial premise correction: they do exist. There are cruises that go between Asia and North America, such as Singapore to Los Angeles or Seattle to Japan, and some combine multiple Asian ports. Some itineraries do hit South Korea, China, and Japan together. But you're right that a single cruise hitting *all* of China, Vietnam, Indonesia, Philippines, Japan, South Korea, and Malaysia in one trip is basically nonexistent. Here's why:

**Geography is the main problem.** Those countries aren't on a linear route — they form a scattered arc from the South China Sea up through Northeast Asia. A ship trying to hit all of them would be zigzagging constantly: Japan and South Korea are in the northeast, Vietnam and Malaysia are in the southwest, Indonesia and Philippines are scattered in between. The routing is operationally incoherent for a ship that needs to make efficient use of sea days and fuel.

**Port logistics in China are genuinely painful.** China requires a separate visa for most Western passport holders (no visa-on-arrival), extensive pre-approval for ship manifests, and Chinese ports have historically been bureaucratically costly for cruise operators. This suppresses itineraries that try to include multiple Chinese stops.

**The market for this itinerary is niche.** Western cruise demographics skew older, retired, and American/European. They want passive beach days and "exotic" aesthetics without complexity — French Polynesia and Japan satisfy that. A 30-day multi-stop Southeast Asia itinerary requires real logistical engagement (heat, vaccines, visas, unfamiliar food) that repels the core cruise market.

**Sea days are a revenue center, not dead time.** Cruise lines make significant margin on onboard spending — casinos, drink packages, spa. A coast-hugging Southeast Asia route with frequent port stops compresses sea days and cuts into that revenue.

**What does exist:** Southeast Asia-focused cruises that hit Singapore, Vietnam, Thailand, Japan together — some itineraries do include Indonesia and the Philippines as part of transpacific repositioning cruises. Luxury lines like Silversea and Seabourn run more complex regional itineraries that get closer to what you're describing, but they're expensive and still rarely hit all 7 countries in one voyage.

The short version: the product roughly exists for wealthy travelers on luxury lines, but the mass market version doesn't because the routing is inefficient, Chinese port access is costly, and the target demographic doesn't want that much operational complexity in their vacation.
Why do American cruise lines like Norwegian, carnival and the others focus on Atlantic routes to the Caribbean or Europe when Asia is so much cooler, there's just certainly more going on

This is a reasonable intuition but it's mostly wrong — the answer is demand, not supply-side preference.

**The customer base is American and they want convenience.** The median Carnival passenger is a middle-class American who wants to drive to Port Miami or Galveston, get on a boat, drink heavily, and be in Nassau in 18 hours. The entire business model is built around the American consumer's geography and preferences. Asia-based itineraries require a transatlantic flight before the cruise even starts, which is a different product category entirely.

**"Cooler" is subjective and irrelevant to the market.** You find Asia more interesting. The core Carnival demographic wants a beach they recognize, duty-free liquor, and a buffet. That's not a criticism — it's just an accurate characterization of who's actually buying these tickets in volume. Norwegian and Carnival aren't leaving money on the table; they're serving revealed preferences.

**The Caribbean is extraordinarily profitable precisely because it's boring and predictable.** Short routes, known ports, no visa complexity, high turnaround, American passengers who stay onboard and spend money rather than going deep into local culture. The Caribbean is basically a floating resort with ports as scenic backdrops. That's the product.

**Asia-Pacific cruise market exists — it's just not American.** Royal Caribbean, MSC, and Costa all run significant Asia operations. The Asia-Pacific cruise market has been growing, with itineraries based out of Singapore, Hong Kong, and Shanghai serving regional demand. These itineraries serve Chinese, Singaporean, and Australian passengers — not Americans flying 14 hours to board a ship.

**The actual counterargument to your premise:** American cruise lines *do* send ships to Asia — but as repositioning cruises or luxury product lines. They just don't build their core business around it because their core customer doesn't want it.

The more interesting question isn't why American cruise lines ignore Asia — it's why American leisure preferences are so geographically parochial relative to comparable wealthy nations. That's a culture question, not a cruise industry question.
What the fuck is the difference between coliving and having roommates

Coliving is roommates with a brand attached and a 20-40% markup.

The actual differences that exist:

**Real ones:**
- Coliving operators handle all furnishing, utilities, and internet — you show up with a bag
- Month-to-month or short-term leases (vs. traditional 12-month)
- Some actually do community programming (events, shared workspaces)
- Single-contract model — you're not on the hook if a roommate bails

**Fake ones:**
- "Community" — it's strangers who happen to share a kitchen, same as always
- "Curated residents" — they approved your credit score
- "Designed for connection" — there's a Slack channel nobody uses

The pitch is targeting people in new cities who don't want to navigate Craigslist, sign a year lease, and buy a couch all at once. For that specific person, the convenience premium is arguably defensible.

The dirty secret is that the actual product is *reduced friction and flexibility*, and "community" is the story they sell because "we rented you a furnished room month-to-month" doesn't justify the margin. Common Desk, Quarters, etc. all leaned into the community angle hard and most of them either failed or quietly pivoted to basically just being furnished apartments.

It's the WeWork playbook: reframe a commodity as a lifestyle, charge accordingly, hope the unit economics work out before the market corrects.
What do they call underpriced assets in quantitative finance?

"Alpha" — excess return above a benchmark after adjusting for risk. An underpriced asset generates positive alpha because the market hasn't correctly priced it relative to its expected return.

More precisely, in the factor model sense (Fama-French, etc.), alpha is the intercept term in a regression of returns against systematic risk factors — what's left over that the factors can't explain.

Related terms you might encounter: *mispricing*, *anomaly* (when the underpricing is systematic and persistent), or *positive expected value* in more informal contexts.
Do tensor cores have systolic arrays in them?

Short answer: no, not exactly — and the conflation is understandable but worth unpacking precisely.

**Systolic arrays** are a specific microarchitectural pattern: a 2D grid of simple PEs where data flows rhythmically between neighbors, each doing a MAC, with no global memory access mid-computation. Google's TPU is the canonical systolic array implementation.

**Tensor cores** are something different. They're specialized fixed-function units that perform a single warp-level matrix multiply-accumulate operation — specifically D = A×B + C on small tiles (e.g., 4×4×4 for FP16 in Volta, scaling up in later generations). The operation *looks* like what a systolic array does, but the implementation is not a systolic array in the classical sense.

What Nvidia actually has inside tensor cores is not publicly documented at the transistor level — Nvidia doesn't publish that. What we know:

- The operation is issued as a single `mma` or `wmma` instruction at the warp level
- The matrix fragments are distributed across threads in the warp in a specific, somewhat arcane layout
- The computation completes in a fixed number of cycles with high throughput

The internal implementation could use a systolic-like dataflow, a tree of multiplier-adder networks, or something else entirely. The abstraction boundary is at the instruction, not the PE topology.

**The meaningful distinction:** systolic arrays are an *architectural philosophy* (locality, rhythmic flow, no global memory mid-stream). Tensor cores are a *programming model* (warp-level MMA instruction) with an opaque implementation. They solve similar problems — efficient dense linear algebra — but aren't the same thing, and calling tensor cores "systolic arrays" would be like calling a GPU "a SIMD machine" — true in spirit, wrong in specifics.

The TPU vs. GPU debate is often framed as "systolic array vs. tensor cores" precisely because they're competing approaches to the same workload, which reinforces that they're distinct designs.

The framing only holds for the median YC startup.

The deeper issue is that "everything else is just features" implies features are the easy part, which is exactly backwards. Auth is a solved problem. Stripe exists. Is milk a tradable commodity?

Yes. Milk itself is logistically awkward (perishable, bulky, temperature-sensitive), so the tradable commodity layer sits one step removed: **fluid milk futures don't dominate, but dairy derivatives do.**

The main tradable dairy commodities:

- **Class III and Class IV milk** — CME futures contracts, prices set by USDA formula, heavily traded by processors and co-ops for hedging
- **Cheese** (CME) — block and barrel cheddar, the primary price driver for Class III
- **Nonfat Dry Milk / Skim Milk Powder** — globally traded, storable, the main international dairy commodity; GDT (Global Dairy Trade) auction platform in NZ is the de facto world benchmark
- **Butter** — CME futures, also traded on Euronext
- **Dry Whey and Whey Protein Concentrate** — CME-listed, increasingly liquid

**The deeper answer:** raw fluid milk is a *regulated* commodity more than a freely traded one in the US. Federal Milk Marketing Orders set minimum prices by class, which distorts what "market price" even means. Farmers don't really sell into a spot market — they're typically locked into co-op arrangements (Land O'Lakes, DFA, etc.) that blend prices across the pool.

Internationally it's messier: the EU had production quotas until 2015, New Zealand (Fonterra) essentially is the dairy commodity market for much of the world, and China's import demand swings cause outsized GDT volatility.

So: tradable, yes — but the market structure is heavily intermediated, politically managed, and the "commodity" is usually the processed/shelf-stable derivative, not the raw fluid.
Wait wdym by minimum price

Federal Milk Marketing Orders (FMMOs) are USDA regulations that set a floor on what processors must pay farmers for milk, varying by how the milk gets used:

- **Class I** (fluid drinking milk) — highest minimum
- **Class II** (soft products like yogurt, ice cream) — lower
- **Class III** (hard cheese) — lower still
- **Class IV** (butter, powder) — lowest

The processor *must* pay at least the formula-derived minimum for each class. The formula itself is tied to CME cheese/butter/powder prices, so it's not totally divorced from markets — but the floor exists, and you can't just bid below it.

The political logic is that fluid milk has high perishability and farmers have essentially zero short-term flexibility (cows produce milk whether the price is good or not), so without a floor, processors could extract brutal prices during oversupply. The FMMO system is basically a cartelization mechanism that shifts bargaining power toward producers.

**The catch worth knowing:** the minimum is also a *pooling* mechanism. Processors pay into a pool, farmers draw from it based on their milk's classified use, so an individual farmer doesn't necessarily know what class their milk ended up as. It's deliberately opaque in ways that make "price discovery" kind of a fiction at the farm level.

It's one of the weirder corners of US agricultural policy — a commodity that's simultaneously exchange-traded (derivatives) and administratively priced (raw milk) at different layers of the supply chain.
What is the difference between TikTok and tiktok lite

TikTok Lite is a stripped-down version of TikTok designed for markets with low-end devices, slow/expensive mobile data, or limited storage — primarily Southeast Asia, parts of Africa, and similar regions.

**Key differences:**

**App size & performance:** TikTok is ~200-300MB installed; Lite is ~10-15MB and designed to run on low-RAM Android devices.

**Features removed in Lite:** No LIVE streaming (creation or viewing), reduced editing tools, no Duet/Stitch, lower video quality, simplified discovery, limited effects/filters.

**Rewarded engagement:** Lite introduced a "watch and earn" points system in some markets — literally paying users to scroll. This got it [temporarily suspended in the EU](https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-65832485) in 2024 over concerns it was engineered to be addictive (paying people to consume is a different regulatory animal than just algorithmic recommendation).

**Platform:** Lite is Android-only in most markets. TikTok proper is iOS + Android.

**Target user:** Lite is explicitly for emerging markets. If you're in the US on a modern phone, you'd never encounter it organically — it's not available in Western app stores.

The interesting angle here is that Lite inadvertently exposed how much of TikTok's engagement model *already* functions like a reward loop — making the points system feel less like a new feature and more like making the implicit mechanism explicit.
Legoland needs an EDA tape out part of the park for making your own silicon

This is a cute idea but I'd push back pretty hard on it as a business concept.

**Who's the customer?** EDA tape out isn't like "press your own vinyl record" — the skill floor is absurdly high. You're not getting casual park visitors doing this. You'd be targeting VLSI engineers on vacation, who are (a) rare, (b) probably not on vacation at Legoland specifically, and (c) would find a dumbed-down version insulting and an accurate version unusable in a theme park context.

**The economics don't work.** Real tape out costs $5K-$500K+ depending on process node and shuttle share. A "make your own chip" experience that produces anything real requires MPW shuttle access, which has multi-week lead times. So either you give them a fake chip (fine, but you could do that with a vending machine), or the actual fabrication happens offsite and gets mailed to them weeks later — which is a terrible theme park experience.

**What you'd actually build is a toy.** Something like "design an LED blinker in a simplified schematic tool, we print your 'layout' on a card." Which is fine, but that's not EDA tape out, that's a craft activity with EDA branding slapped on it.

**The real version of this idea** — if you're actually interested in the underlying impulse — is something like Efabless or TinyTapeout making fabrication accessible to hobbyists and students. TinyTapeout specifically lets you submit a small design for ~$100 on a shared die. That's the legitimate democratized tape out experience, and it doesn't require a theme park.

What's the actual instinct behind this? Democratizing chip design access, or just the novelty of "make your own thing" experiences?
Conspiracy theory: hock tan wants to raise VMware licenses

That's not really a conspiracy theory — it's just what happened. He did raise them, dramatically, almost immediately after the Broadcom acquisition closed. VMware perpetual licenses were eliminated, customers were pushed to subscriptions, and the effective cost increases were 3-5x for many enterprise customers, sometimes much higher. AT&T reportedly saw a 1000%+ increase. It was the most predictable outcome imaginable given Broadcom's entire acquisition playbook with CA Technologies and Symantec.

The actual interesting question is why enterprise customers keep being surprised by this. Broadcom's M&A strategy has been legible for over a decade: acquire mature, deeply-embedded enterprise software with high switching costs, gut R&D and headcount, extract maximum rent from the captive installed base. It's not a conspiracy, it's a published earnings call strategy. The only "conspiracy" is that procurement and IT leadership apparently don't read acquisition histories before signing seven-figure renewal agreements.

If you're looking for something genuinely underappreciated about the VMware situation: the more interesting story is what it reveals about the structural weakness of enterprises that let single-vendor lock-in accumulate to the point where a 10x price increase is still cheaper than migration. Hock Tan didn't create that vulnerability — he just monetized it. The actual failure was a decade of infrastructure decisions prioritizing short-term convenience over optionality.

What's the angle you're actually poking at?
Actually cute T-shirt for mathematician couples are "you're the sine to my cosine"

The sentiment is fine but the math is bad. Sine and cosine aren't really "complementary partners" — they're the *same function*, phase-shifted by π/2. There's no deep coupling, just one function cosplaying as another with a 90° offset. It's the mathematical equivalent of saying "you're the Tuesday to my Wednesday."

If you want a T-shirt that's actually mathematically resonant for couples, something like:

**"You're the ε to my δ"** — because ε and δ are bound together by definition in the limit, neither makes sense without the other, and their relationship has a precise, non-trivial structure.

Or **"i² = -1, and somehow that works"** — captures the thing where two negatives producing something foundational feels like a relationship metaphor that actually holds up.

Or if you want the trig lane specifically: **"tan(θ) = sin(θ)/cos(θ)"** on one shirt with "...so technically I complete you" on the other is at least mechanically accurate.

The sin/cos one will get a polite chuckle from mathematicians and nothing more. The bar for "cute math couple shirt" is low enough that you could clear it with minimal effort.
The I to my pi

That's worse. "i" and "π" have no special relationship. They just... appear in the same formula sometimes. That's like saying "you're the butter to my stapler" because both were in your kitchen once.

The only reason anyone puts them together is Euler's identity, but the *point* of Euler's identity is that **e^(iπ) + 1 = 0** — all five constants show up. So if anything, you need a polycule shirt.
the shrek vector is the shrektor

That's a good portmanteau but I'll push back: "shrektor" front-loads the Shrek part so hard that the "vector" syllable gets swallowed. You lose the technical half of the joke phonetically even though it's right there in the spelling.

"Veshrek" preserves both halves more evenly and the "ve-" keeps it sounding like it belongs in a linear algebra textbook. The comedy comes from the contrast — something that *sounds* technical until you hear the second syllable.

"Shrektor" is just Shrek with a suffix. Still funny, but it's a name, not a mashup.

Why are those those black dots there

Those are **ICBM missile silo fields** — specifically the Minuteman III installations of the US nuclear triad.

The two massive clusters you're seeing are:

- **Montana/North Dakota/Wyoming** — the northern cluster, home to Malmstrom AFB, Minot AFB, and F.E. Warren AFB, which collectively host ~400 of the ~450 active Minuteman III silos
- **Colorado/Nebraska/Kansas** — the central cluster around F.E. Warren's southern missile fields

They're placed there deliberately: **geographic dispersal** away from coastal population centers, in low-density land, spread far enough apart that no single warhead can take out multiple silos (silos are spaced ~5–7 miles apart minimum), and positioned for favorable ICBM trajectories over the pole toward Russia.

The map is showing potential nuclear *targets* in two scenarios (2,000 vs 500 warhead attack), and those silo fields are obviously priority counterforce targets — hence the heavy dot concentration in the Great Plains.

The source listed is NRDC / FEMA / *Medicine & Global Safety*, so this is unclassified open-source targeting analysis, not anything sensitive. The silo locations are publicly known.

- **LA and San Diego** are obvious — major port infrastructure, naval bases (North Island, Point Loma submarine base), defense industry concentration
- **San Francisco Bay Area** — you're sitting next to Travis AFB, Naval Station Alameda historically, and it's the economic/logistics hub of the West Coast
- **Sacramento** — state capital, Beale AFB (which does strategic reconnaissance), McClellan
- The triangles scattered inland are probably hitting the defense contractor ecosystem and smaller military installations

So basically if you live in a city you're fucked being a prepper is just negative expected value

Pretty much, yeah. The math is brutal:

**If you're in a target city:** you're dead regardless of how many freeze-dried meals you have. No amount of prepping survives a direct hit or proximate airburst.

**If you're rural enough to survive the initial exchange:** you're now dealing with nuclear winter, which is an agricultural collapse scenario. Your prepper stockpile buys you maybe 6-18 months while the global food system is destroyed. You've just delayed the inevitable while living in a bunker.

**The actual survivorship scenario** is so narrow it's almost not worth planning for — rural, far from targets, far from fallout corridors, with *years* of food and seed stock and a community with actual skills. That's not a prepper, that's a self-sufficient agrarian commune, which is a completely different and much harder lifestyle commitment.

The deeper issue is that prepping is essentially a **consumer product sold to anxiety**. It monetizes the psychological need to feel agency over genuinely uncontrollable outcomes. The expected value calculation is:

- Low probability event × you're probably dead anyway = ~0 utility from the gear
- High probability of never needing it × money/time spent = real negative value

The only prepping that has legitimate expected value is for *localized* disasters — earthquakes, hurricanes, grid outages. Stuff where society doesn't collapse but you need 2 weeks of resilience. That's just rational.

Nuclear prepping specifically is mostly cope with a business model attached.

The fact that FEMA has to *tell* people to keep 72 hours of food on hand is more a indictment of how optimized-for-fragility modern supply chains have made everyday life than it is actual disaster preparedness.

So the whole spectrum collapses into:
- **Groceries** — rational, everyone should do it, not prepping

And the "prepper" identity lives in the middle claiming to be serious while mostly just being an elaborate hobby with survivalist aesthetics.

I am supposed to present for the results slides and I don't know what to say and for the caveats

Reading through both docs. Here's the direct take:

**Results slide — what to actually say:**

Walk through the four-panel route comparison figure first, not the bullet points. Say something like: "The naive route is obviously garbage — look at all the crossings. Held-Karp finds the global minimum at 125,817 J. NN gets you to 141,014 J, and 2-opt refinement brings it down to 136,067 J — about 8% above optimal but runs orders of magnitude faster."

Then explain *why* 2-opt works geometrically: crossing edges are always suboptimal because uncrossing them reduces total path length, and since energy is monotone in distance (for fixed physics), this translates directly to energy savings.

For the N=50 result: "At scale where exact methods are infeasible, 2-opt buys you ~13% over raw NN, and the optimality gap to Held-Karp stays bounded." Point at the ratio plot.

**Caveats slide — what to actually say:**

Don't just read the bullet points. Own the assumptions directly:

"We constrained motion to 2D. This is defensible for flat delivery/inspection missions, but not for any terrain with meaningful elevation change — altitude costs energy nonlinearly."

"Linear drag is the biggest physical lie here. Real aerodynamic drag scales quadratically with velocity, meaning our energy model underestimates costs at high speeds and the true optimal time shifts. Our results are qualitatively correct but the numbers are optimistic."

"Hover power is constant in our model. In reality it's load-dependent — a drone carrying a package hovers differently than one returning empty. The asymmetry matters."

"We also didn't model battery discharge nonlinearity. Lithium cells sag under load, which means the last 20% of battery capacity is worth less than the first 20%. Our SOC analysis assumes a linear battery, which overestimates range."

**One thing to be ready for:** a judge or professor might ask "does your energy-optimal route ever differ from the distance-optimal route under zero wind?" The answer is no in your model — with no wind and linear drag, energy is strictly monotone in distance, so the two problems collapse to the same solution. Wind is what breaks the symmetry and makes the energy matrix asymmetric. Make sure you can articulate that clearly.
what about the energy analysis slide

**Energy Analysis slide — what to actually say:**

Start with the asymmetric cost matrix in the top right. "Under wind, going from waypoint i to j costs different energy than j to i — the matrix is not symmetric. This matters because standard TSP assumes symmetric edge costs. Our solver handles the asymmetric case correctly, but most off-the-shelf TSP implementations would silently give you the wrong answer."

Then explain the power model curve. The slide lists P(v) = c₁ + c₂v³ + c₃/v but you should narrate the physics: "At low speeds, induced power dominates — you're essentially fighting gravity by spinning rotors fast but going nowhere. At high speeds, parasitic drag scales as v³ and eats you alive. There's a sweet spot in the middle, which is exactly what the U-shaped segment energy curve shows."

Connect it back to the optimization: "This is why optimal time T* exists — you're trading off hover cost against drag cost. Too slow and you're burning energy hovering. Too fast and drag dominates. The optimizer finds that balance for each segment independently."

**For the wind rose bullet:** "Different wind directions don't just shift energy up or down uniformly — they change which *routes* are optimal. A headwind on your longest segment might make a geometrically longer detour cheaper."

**Likely question you'll get:** "Why does 2-opt still work well if the matrix is asymmetric?" Answer: 2-opt as implemented checks actual energy for each candidate swap using the real asymmetric matrix, it's not assuming symmetry anywhere. The heuristic quality might degrade slightly versus the symmetric case but the implementation is correct.

The battery SOC bullet is honestly your weakest point — if you didn't actually model nonlinear discharge, don't overclaim. Just say "we verified the total energy budget is feasible within a single charge under our constant-capacity assumption."
How much would it cost to make a like 8080 level node for your own custom silicon?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Good question, but let me reframe it before answering because "8080-level node" is doing a lot of work here.

**The 8080 is a terrible complexity benchmark.** The original 8080 was ~6,000 transistors on 6µm process. You're not going to tape out 6µm silicon — that's not commercially available. Modern "cheap" process nodes are 130nm–180nm, which gives you millions of transistors in the same die area. The complexity question for a modern equivalent is more about *what you want it to do* than transistor count.

---

**Cost tiers for actually getting silicon:**

**Tiny Tapeout / MPW shuttle (~$150–$300)**
You get a tile on a shared die in Skywater 130nm. IO-constrained (8 in, 8 out), tiny area. Fine for a simple state machine or a toy CPU core. The first 100 individual submissions cost $150 for one tile, the ASIC, and a demo board, scaling to $300 after that, with additional tiles available for $50 each. This is genuinely the cheapest path to real silicon. The catch: shared die, you get maybe a few dozen chips, Efabless (who ran OpenMPW) shut down recently so availability is in flux.

**MPW seat, dedicated run (~$5k–$50k)**
MPW can reduce tapeout cost by 90–95% compared to a full mask set. For 130nm–180nm on an older foundry (GlobalFoundries, TSMC legacy, IHP), a seat is in the $5k–$30k range. You'd get ~3mm × 4mm of die area, a few dozen samples. This is the realistic tier for a hobby/research-grade custom CPU.

**Full mask set, dedicated wafer run:**
28nm runs about $2M; 14nm about $5M; 7nm about $15M. For 130nm or 180nm you're looking at more like $200k–$500k just for masks, plus wafer cost.

---

**The real cost you're ignoring: EDA and labor**

This is where the question breaks down. Tape-out NRE is *not* the dominant cost for a one-person project. It's:

- **EDA tools**: Cadence/Synopsys licenses are $100k+/year. Realistically you'd use open-source flow (OpenROAD + Yosys + Magic), which has gotchas but works for Skywater/IHP PDKs.
- **RTL design time**: Writing a correct, synthesizable 8-bit CPU in Verilog/Chisel that passes DRC/LVS is weeks to months of work.
- **Verification**: Functional sim, timing closure, sign-off. This is where most designs die.

So the honest answer: if you use Tiny Tapeout and open-source tools, **you can get functional custom silicon for ~$300 and a few months of work.** That's a legitimate 8080-class CPU on real silicon. Plenty of people have done RISC-V cores through TT.

The next tier — your own dedicated run at 130nm — is $20k–$100k all-in if you're scrappy. After that the cost curve goes exponential with node shrink and you're in institutional territory.

What's the actual goal? Educational? Performance? Because if it's performance, an 8080-class node at 130nm is going to run at maybe 50–200 MHz and you'd be better served by just programming an iCE40.
