Found your problem. Look at the asterisk — you're connected to:

```
* 20:CC:27:8B:49:71  eduroam  channel 11  130 Mbit/s  signal 54
```

But there's a better eduroam AP sitting right there:

```
00:BE:75:21:25:C1  eduroam  channel 11  195 Mbit/s  signal 69
```

Signal 69 vs 54, higher bitrate, same channel. Your device is stubbornly holding onto a worse AP when a better one is available. This is the classic Linux WiFi roaming problem — the mt7925 driver's roaming aggressiveness is too conservative compared to how MacBooks handle it. macOS roams proactively; Linux tends to hang on to whatever it connected to first until signal completely falls off a cliff.

**Also notice:** everything you're seeing is 2.4GHz (channels 1, 6, 11) or low 5GHz. The MT7925 is a Wi-Fi 7 chip — it should be capable of 6GHz. UCSC may not have 6GHz deployed, but you're also not seeing high 5GHz channels (channels 36, 40 are 5GHz — you see a couple but they're weaker). Not the main issue but worth noting.

**Fix the roaming problem:**

```bash
# Check current roaming aggressiveness (0-7, higher = roams more aggressively)
sudo iw dev wlan0 get roam_threshold

# Force a roam to the better AP right now
sudo iwconfig wlan0 ap 00:BE:75:21:25:C1
```

For a persistent fix, tune the roaming threshold via NetworkManager:

```bash
sudo nano /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/wifi-roam.conf
```

Add:
```ini
[connection]
wifi.roam-threshold=70
```

Then:
```bash
sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager
```

This tells NetworkManager to roam when signal drops below 70 (out of 100), which is more aggressive than the default. Your MacBook-using peers get this behavior for free because Apple tunes it well out of the box.
Holy shit that is stupid, there must be a ton of things that are like this

Yeah, and this is actually one of the more benign ones. The WiFi roaming thing is at least fixable with a config knob. A lot of Linux networking decisions are just... historically bad defaults that never got cleaned up because the people who knew enough to fix them already had workarounds.

A few other ones in the same vein:

**TCP congestion control defaults to CUBIC** when BBR has been available since kernel 4.9 and is objectively better on most modern networks. Nobody switched the default.

**DNS resolution order** is a mess — depending on your distro you might have systemd-resolved, resolvconf, and NetworkManager all fighting over `/etc/resolv.conf` simultaneously. MacOS just works. Linux requires you to understand which of three competing systems is currently winning.

**Power management on WiFi is on by default** and will throttle your adapter to save battery even on a plugged-in desktop:
```bash
iwconfig wlan0 | grep Power
# probably says "Power Management:on"
sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off
```

That alone can cause intermittent latency spikes people mistake for signal problems.

**IRQ affinity for network interfaces** is essentially never set optimally out of the box, so on a busy machine all your network interrupts pile onto core 0.

The pattern is: Linux defaults were set in an era of different hardware and different network conditions, kernel devs are conservative about changing defaults because something somewhere will break, and so bad defaults calcify for years. MacOS has the advantage of Apple controlling the full stack and actually caring about the out-of-box experience for normal users.

You hit one of maybe a dozen papercuts in this category just from using WiFi.
What other issues does linux have that I should be aware of?

Depends heavily on your use case. "Issues" covers a lot of ground — are you talking about:
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Q: What areas matter most to you? (Select all that apply)
A: Daily driver / usability papercuts, Networking / clusters, ML/GPU workloads

Good set. These actually have meaningful overlap — a lot of ML/GPU cluster pain is networking pain. Let me go through each:

---

## Daily Driver Papercuts

**Suspend/resume is a crapshoot.** Especially on newer hardware. MT7925 specifically has known suspend/resume bugs where the WiFi adapter doesn't reinitialize cleanly. If your WiFi dies after waking from sleep:
```bash
sudo systemctl edit systemd-suspend.service
# add a pre-sleep hook to rmmod mt7925e and post-sleep to modprobe it back
```

**Font rendering is subtly worse than macOS.** FreeType hinting defaults are conservative for patent reasons that expired years ago but nobody updated the defaults. Fix:
```bash
sudo dpkg-reconfigure fontconfig
# enable subpixel rendering and autohinter
```

**Bluetooth and WiFi interference** — both on the same chip on your machine (MT7925 is combo). Linux coexistence handling between BT and WiFi on combo chips is worse than Windows/macOS. If you use Bluetooth headphones you'll notice WiFi degradation.

**Filesystem performance defaults** — `ext4` with `relatime` is fine, but if you're on a laptop and didn't explicitly set `noatime`, you're doing unnecessary writes on every file read. Minor but real.

**OOM killer is stupid.** When memory pressure hits, Linux's OOM killer will frequently kill the wrong process — often something important — instead of the actual memory hog. `earlyoom` or `systemd-oomd` with proper configuration is a significant quality of life improvement:
```bash
sudo apt install earlyoom
sudo systemctl enable --now earlyoom
```

---

## Networking / Clusters

This is where things get genuinely painful.

**ECMP hashing is often misconfigured by default.** Equal-cost multipath routing should distribute flows across links, but the default hash parameters frequently result in poor distribution — you'll have one link saturated and others idle. Fixable but requires knowing to look.

**RoCEv2/RDMA — the defaults are basically hostile.** If you're running anything on Nautilus or similar:
- PFC (Priority Flow Control) needs to be explicitly configured or you get head-of-line blocking that tanks throughput
- ECN thresholds are rarely tuned — you'll hit congestion before the network thinks you are
- `mlx5` driver has a graveyard of parameters that matter enormously and are documented only in Mellanox PDFs from 2019

**NIC interrupt coalescing defaults are too aggressive** for latency-sensitive workloads:
```bash
ethtool -c eth0  # check current coalescing
ethtool -C eth0 rx-usecs 50  # tune down for lower latency
```

**TCP buffer sizes don't scale to high-bandwidth links by default.** On a 100GbE link the default TCP window is embarrassingly small:
```bash
sysctl net.core.rmem_max  # probably 212992 — way too small
# should be 134217728 for high throughput
```

**NetworkManager and systemd-networkd will fight each other** if both are active, which is the default on some distros. Silent packet drops, weird routing table entries, fun times.

---

## ML / GPU Workloads

This is the deepest rabbit hole.

**CUDA context initialization is slow and nobody talks about it.** First kernel launch on a fresh process pays a ~1-2 second penalty for JIT compilation of PTX to SASS. If you're benchmarking and not accounting for this you're measuring the wrong thing. Use `CUDA_MODULE_LOADING=EAGER` to front-load it.

**GPU persistence mode is off by default:**
```bash
sudo nvidia-smi -pm 1
```
Without this, the GPU fully reinitializes on every job. On a shared cluster this means every job pays ~10 seconds of overhead and power state transitions cause weird thermal behavior.

**NCCL defaults are conservative.** `NCCL_SOCKET_NTHREADS`, `NCCL_NSOCKS_PERTHREAD`, and the algorithm selection (`NCCL_ALGO`) all have defaults tuned for generic hardware. On a specific cluster topology — especially if you have NVLink within nodes and RoCE between nodes — the defaults will leave significant bandwidth on the table. NCCL will often pick Ring when Tree is faster, or vice versa.

**NUMA topology is almost never respected.** If your GPU is on NUMA node 1 and you're allocating CPU memory from node 0, you're going through QPI/UPI for every H2D transfer. `numactl` exists but almost nobody uses it. On multi-socket systems this is a real performance hit.

**MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) partitioning** is off by default on A100s/H100s and requires a full driver restart to change — meaning whoever set up the cluster made a one-time decision that affects everyone, often wrong.

**Power limits are frequently set wrong** on cluster GPUs. An A100 SXM can draw 400W but clusters often cap it at 300W for thermal/power budget reasons without telling you. You're leaving compute on the table and don't know it:
```bash
nvidia-smi -q -d POWER  # check current limit vs max
```

---

The common thread across all three: Linux gives you all the knobs but ships with defaults that made sense in 2008 or were set conservatively to avoid breaking edge cases, and nobody's job is to audit them for your specific workload. MacOS and Windows hide the knobs but at least tune the defaults for normal use. On a cluster the problem compounds because you're inheriting every bad default decision made by whoever set it up.

Given you're working on Nautilus specifically, the NCCL and RoCEv2 stuff is probably the highest leverage for your actual work.

Honestly? For your use case, not the wrong call.

The people who benefit most from Linux as a daily driver are either sysadmins who live in the terminal and don't care about the desktop, or people running servers where there's no desktop at all. For someone doing ML systems work on a laptop who also wants WiFi to just work and suspend/resume to not be a dice roll — macOS is a genuinely better experience and it's not close.

The counterarguments people usually make:

**"But you have full control on Linux"** — true, but you're spending that control budget fixing defaults that shouldn't need fixing instead of doing actual work. The WiFi roaming thing you hit today is a perfect example. That's an hour of your life gone.

**"MacBooks can't run CUDA"** — correct, but you're doing your actual GPU work on Nautilus anyway. Your laptop is a client to the cluster, not the compute node. You don't need CUDA on the laptop. You need a terminal, VS Code, and a browser that doesn't eat your battery.

**"Linux is free"** — you're a senior at UCSC, presumably have access to student discounts, and the time cost of fighting Linux papercuts is not free.

The one real argument for staying on Linux as a daily driver in your position: the forced familiarity with the system internals is genuinely useful for ML systems work. Knowing why NUMA topology matters because you got burned by it on your own machine is different from reading about it. Some of what you know about networking came directly from debugging your own WiFi today.

But that's a pretty expensive way to learn, and at some point you've internalized enough that the marginal return on daily Linux pain drops off. Sounds like you might be approaching that point.

Exactly. And Kubernetes arguably surfaces the painful ones more nakedly than desktop Linux does — you'll fight NUMA topology, NCCL tuning, RoCEv2 PFC misconfiguration, and OOM killer misbehavior in a context where it actually costs real compute time on a shared cluster, not just mildly annoys you on your laptop.

The desktop Linux papercuts are just noise on top of that. Fixing your WiFi roaming threshold has zero transfer value to debugging why your NCCL allreduce is underperforming on Nautilus. Whereas the hour you would have spent on that is an hour you could have spent actually understanding the cluster issues that matter for Firing Pin or DIME.

There's also a subtle thing where fighting your tools creates a low-grade cognitive tax that's easy to underestimate. Every time your WiFi drops or suspend breaks you context-switch out of whatever you were actually thinking about. On a MacBook that stuff just doesn't happen, which means more of your mental budget goes toward the actual hard problems.

Get the MacBook, put Linux in a VM or just SSH into Nautilus for anything that needs it, and stop paying the daily driver tax.
So this is why people buy macs because stuff just works

Yeah, that's genuinely it. The "it just works" thing isn't marketing fluff — it's the result of Apple controlling the full stack from silicon to OS to driver to application framework. When something breaks they can fix it end to end without waiting on three different open source maintainers to coordinate.

The WiFi thing is a perfect microcosm. Your situation involved:
- MediaTek writing a driver
- The kernel team merging and maintaining it
- NetworkManager setting roaming defaults
- Your distro packaging all of it together

Nobody in that chain is responsible for the end-to-end experience. Apple has one team that owns all of it and is incentivized to make it not embarrass them in reviews.

The reason this wasn't obvious historically is Apple hardware used to be significantly more expensive for equivalent specs, so the "tax" was real. That calculus shifted a lot with M-series chips — you're now getting better performance per watt than comparable x86 laptops, not just better software integration. The M4 MacBook Pro benchmarks are genuinely absurd for the price point.

The people still on Linux laptops by choice at this point are mostly either ideologically committed to open source, locked into a specific workflow that requires it, or haven't done the math on how much time they're spending on maintenance. You clearly aren't any of those things.

The irony for your specific career path is that the ML systems and infrastructure world runs almost entirely on Linux — but on servers, not laptops. Your MacBook will SSH into Linux machines all day. Best of both worlds.
I suppose my next laptop will be a mac then because I just bought this framework last year and it is supposed to last me a while because of the repairability of it

That's a completely reasonable position — a Framework is probably the best possible argument for staying on Linux on a laptop. The whole value prop is longevity and repairability, so replacing it after a year would defeat the purpose entirely.

And Framework's Linux support is actually better than most — they actively maintain compatibility, publish guides, and their community is much more responsive to driver issues than a typical OEM. So the practical path is probably: fix the papercuts that are actually bothering you now (the roaming threshold you already fixed is a good start, kill WiFi power management, set up the suspend/resume hook), learn to ignore the rest, and reassess when the Framework actually dies or becomes inadequate for your workload.

By the time you're buying your next laptop you'll likely be post-graduation, potentially at a job that hands you a MacBook anyway. A lot of the places you're targeting — Anyscale, Modal, Databricks — just give engineers MacBooks and the question becomes moot.

The Framework was probably the right buy for a broke undergrad who needs repairability. The Mac will be the right buy for the person you are in two years.
Knowledge representation in first order logic have stuff like soundness and like 4 other properties I forget

The standard properties for a knowledge representation system (Levesque & Brachman's framework) are:

**Representational Adequacy** — can it express everything you need to represent? FOL handles this well for most domains but chokes on things like modal reasoning or default/non-monotonic inference without extensions.

**Inferential Adequacy** — can the inference engine derive all entailed conclusions? FOL is complete (Gödel) — anything true is provable in principle.

**Inferential Efficiency** — how fast can it actually derive those conclusions? This is where FOL gets ugly. Full FOL is semi-decidable; propositional logic is decidable but NP-complete. Real systems make tradeoffs here constantly.

**Acquisitional Efficiency** — how easy is it to add new knowledge to the system? This is about practical KB engineering — can you incrementally add facts without blowing everything up?

**Soundness** — the one you remembered. If the system derives X, then X is actually entailed. No false positives from inference.

Some sources also split out **Completeness** separately from inferential adequacy, treating it as: if X is entailed, the system will eventually derive it. FOL is complete but not decidable, so "eventually" can mean infinite loops in practice.

The big tension in KR is always efficiency vs. expressivity. FOL is maximally expressive among classical logics but pays for it with undecidability. Description logics (the basis of OWL) are essentially carefully restricted subsets of FOL designed to recover decidability while keeping enough expressivity to be useful — that tradeoff is basically the whole field.

name: Commercial insurance for drug sales representatives

messages.text: Do you need commercial insurance if you are using your car to drive a lot for being a drug sales rep?

Yes, almost certainly. Here's the actual picture:

**Standard personal auto policies exclude business use** — and "business use" is defined broadly. Driving to client accounts, carrying samples, attending sales calls — that's commercial use, full stop. If you get in an accident while doing that and your insurer figures out it was work-related (and they will look), they can deny the claim entirely.

**What you actually need:**

- **Business use endorsement** — cheapest fix if your insurer offers it, adds coverage for driving for work. Not all insurers offer this.
- **Commercial auto policy** — more comprehensive, covers liability for business driving, often required by the employer anyway.
- **Check your employer first** — many pharma/medical sales companies carry a fleet or umbrella policy that covers employees driving personal vehicles for work. Your HR or benefits team should have this answer.

**The practical reality:** Drug/pharma reps drive a *lot* — often 20,000–40,000 miles/year for work. Insurers who see that mileage on a personal policy are going to ask questions. High mileage alone can trigger scrutiny.

**Bottom line:** Call your insurer, tell them what you do, and ask specifically whether your current policy covers business use driving. Don't bury it — if you're in an accident and they discover it wasn't disclosed, you're looking at a denied claim plus potential policy cancellation. The endorsement or commercial policy typically costs less than people expect.
Do most of them have commerical insurance?

Honestly, the norm varies but here's what's actually typical:

**Most large pharma/medical device companies** (Pfizer, J&J, Medtronic, etc.) carry a **hired and non-owned auto (HNOA) policy** — this covers employees using personal vehicles for company business. So there's *some* employer-side coverage, but it's liability-focused and protects the *company*, not necessarily you fully.

**What that means for you practically:**
- It covers liability to *third parties* if you cause an accident while working
- It often does **not** cover damage to *your own vehicle*
- Your personal policy gap still exists for your own car

**The rep reality on the ground:** A lot of reps are actually underinsured and don't know it. They assume the company's HNOA policy has them covered and never get the business use endorsement on their personal policy. That's a common mistake — if your car gets totaled on a sales call, HNOA isn't paying to fix it.

**What the good employers actually do:** Require you to carry a minimum liability amount (often $100k/$300k or higher) as a condition of employment, and reimburse mileage (which implicitly acknowledges it's business driving).

**What to actually do:** Get the HNOA policy details from your employer in writing, then call your personal insurer and close whatever gap remains. Takes an hour, costs maybe $100-200/year for the endorsement.

Don't assume you're covered just because your company has a policy.

All those results are about undergrad admissions — not what you're asking. Graduate decisions at UCSC are handled entirely differently: there's no system-wide release date. It's program-by-program, department-by-department.

Faculty committees in each department evaluate applications and make admissions recommendations to the Dean of Graduate Studies, who makes final decisions. That decentralized structure means each program notifies applicants on its own timeline, typically anywhere from January through April for fall admits.

Your best bet is to check the specific program's application deadlines page — UCSC Graduate Admissions publishes a deadlines sheet at graduateadmissions.ucsc.edu/application-deadlines/ — and then either email the department directly or check GradCafe for historical data points on when that specific program has sent decisions in past cycles.

Is there a right-wing version of the view for conservative women I mean the thing is that the view seems to be the appearance of disagreement where there seems to be a unanimous agreement on a lot of things

Yeah, your diagnosis is correct — The View's "disagreement" is mostly aesthetic. The Overton window on that show is pretty narrow; the "conservative" seat (historically Elisabeth Hasselbeck, Meghan McCain, Alyssa Farah Griffin) exists largely to be outvoted and occasionally ratio'd, not to actually shift the discourse.

There have been attempts at a right-wing equivalent:

**Closest analog: Outnumbered (Fox News)** — literally a panel format with multiple women and one rotating male "outnumbered." But it has the same structural problem in reverse: the disagreement is performative, consensus is assumed, and the Overton window is just shifted right.

**The View's actual problem** isn't that it's left-coded, it's that it's *consensus entertainment*. The format selects for emotional reaction and group affirmation, not genuine argument. A real conservative version would have the same dysfunction.

**Why this format doesn't really work for genuine conservative women's voices:** The women who have the most interesting right-of-center perspectives — Bari Weiss types, heterodox classical liberals, serious social conservatives — tend to gravitate toward podcasts and Substack rather than daytime TV panels, because the format punishes nuance. The panel show format is structurally optimized for hot takes and solidarity signaling, which is why both The View and Outnumbered feel intellectually hollow.

**The more honest question** is whether the demand is for genuine debate among conservative women, or just a mirror-image affirmation machine for a different audience. Outnumbered pretty clearly chose the latter.

What's drawing you to this question — the media format angle or more the political representation angle?
There is a non zero amount of people who think ice and isis are related somehow

Yeah, this comes up. The theory is basically: ICE (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) → enforces immigration policy → targets undocumented people → therefore comparable to ISIS/ISIL.

It's a bad argument, and it's worth being direct about why.

**The actual connection being claimed:**
- Both use acronyms with "I" — this is noise
- Both are accused of violence/human rights abuses — this is the substantive claim
- Some people genuinely believe ICE's family separation policies and detention conditions constitute state terror comparable to a theocratic militant group that executed journalists and enslaved Yazidis

**Why the comparison fails analytically:**
- ISIS was a non-state actor pursuing a caliphate through mass executions, genocidal ethnic cleansing, and international terrorism. ICE is a federal law enforcement agency operating within a legal framework that can be challenged in court, voted on, and reformed.
- Conflating "policy I find morally abhorrent" with "equivalent to ISIS" destroys the analytical usefulness of both categories.
- It's motivated reasoning — people emotionally committed to the horror of detention conditions reaching for the most viscerally impactful comparison, which actually *weakens* the critique because it's so easily dismissed.

**The legitimate critique underneath it:**
ICE detention conditions, particularly during family separation (2018), generated credible human rights documentation. That case stands on its own without the ISIS comparison, and is *stronger* without it.

The comparison is a rhetorical shortcut that signals tribal affiliation more than it communicates anything analytically useful.
What if a member of isis says "I'm going to blow up the bathroom" and then goes in there and takes a massive poop
The punchline is just the contrast between the extreme threat and the mundane reality. It's a solid joke structure — subverted expectations, the more menacing the setup the better the payoff.
Is porton on macos

No. Porton (the GPU memory profiler from the Bytedance team) is Linux-only — it hooks into CUDA driver internals and uses Linux-specific mechanisms (`/proc`, `LD_PRELOAD` interception, perf events) that don't exist on macOS. macOS dropped CUDA support entirely after 10.13 High Sierra anyway, so there's no CUDA stack to profile.

If you're trying to profile GPU memory on macOS, your options are basically:

- **Metal Performance HUD** — Apple's built-in overlay for Metal GPU stats
- **Instruments (Xcode)** — Metal System Trace for memory and shader profiling
- **XCode GPU Frame Debugger** — for per-frame memory captures

But realistically, if you're doing serious CUDA/PyTorch profiling work (Firing Pin context, Nautilus jobs), you're not doing it on macOS anyway — you'd profile directly on a Linux node with the actual GPU. The dev loop of "profile locally on Mac" doesn't apply here.

What are you actually trying to do?
I meant proton

Still no, not natively — Proton is a Steam Play compatibility layer built on Wine + DXVK/VKD3D, and it's Linux-only by design. The whole point of Proton is bridging Windows games to Linux; macOS already runs games natively or via its own compatibility paths.

Your options on macOS for playing Windows games:

- **CrossOver** (commercial Wine fork, actually works reasonably well)
- **Whisky** (free, open-source Wine wrapper, decent for lighter games)
- **Parallels** (full Windows VM, best compatibility, costs money, ARM translation overhead on Apple Silicon)
- **Boot Camp** (Intel Macs only, dead on Apple Silicon)

Apple Silicon specifically makes this messier — you're running ARM Windows in Parallels translating x86 game code via Rosetta-equivalent, so performance on demanding titles is hit or miss.

What game are you trying to run?
Is nutanix a VMware competitor

Yes, directly. Nutanix is primarily a hyperconverged infrastructure (HCI) vendor that competes with VMware in a few meaningful ways:

**Where they actually compete:**
- Nutanix AHV (their hypervisor) vs VMware vSphere/ESXi
- Nutanix Cloud Infrastructure vs vSAN for storage virtualization
- Both target enterprise on-prem virtualization workloads

**Where the comparison gets messy:**
- Nutanix started as HCI hardware/software, VMware was pure hypervisor — they approached the same customer from different directions
- VMware (now Broadcom-owned) is still the dominant hypervisor by installed base, by a lot. Nutanix is a real alternative but not a peer in market share terms
- A ton of Nutanix deployments *run on top of* VMware — they've historically been partners as much as competitors

**The Broadcom acquisition actually helped Nutanix** — Hock Tan's aggressive licensing changes post-VMware acquisition drove a real wave of customers evaluating exits, and Nutanix was the obvious landing spot. That's probably the most interesting competitive dynamic right now.

**The honest framing:** Nutanix is the most credible VMware alternative for on-prem virtualization, but calling them a "VMware competitor" slightly overstates their position historically. Post-Broadcom chaos, the competitive window is real and Nutanix has been capitalizing on it.

What's the context — evaluating them as a vendor, or something else?

Is there a pipeline from Iran to China for oil?

Yes, but it's not a physical pipeline — it's a maritime shipping route, and a well-established one.

**The actual mechanism:** Iranian crude moves by tanker through the Persian Gulf/Gulf of Oman, around or through the Strait of Malacca, to Chinese ports — primarily Zhoushan, Qingdao, and Tianjin. It's a ~20-25 day voyage.

**Key operational details:**
- Iran is one of China's top oil suppliers, regularly in the top 3-5 despite sanctions
- Trade runs through a shadow fleet of tankers — often flagged under third countries (Panama, Cameroon, etc.), with AIS transponders disabled or spoofed
- Payment happens in yuan or via barter arrangements, deliberately bypassing SWIFT
- Chinese "teapot refineries" (independent refiners in Shandong province) are the primary buyers — they're willing to take the sanctions risk that state-owned refiners won't

**Scale:** China imports roughly 1-1.5 million barrels/day from Iran at peak periods, heavily discounted (sometimes 10-15% below Brent). Post-2018 sanctions reimposition under Trump, this became the backbone of Iran's hard currency earnings.

**No physical pipeline exists** and likely never will — the geography is prohibitive (thousands of miles of Central Asian terrain, multiple hostile/difficult transit states), and the maritime route works well enough that there's no economic case to build one.

There have been loose discussions about overland rail + pipeline concepts through Pakistan or Central Asia, but nothing serious has materialized.

name: Exxon employment and Guyana's economic development

messages.text: Why don't people want to work for Exxon? Do they just don't like guyana economic developement?

The framing here is off. "People don't want to work for Exxon" isn't really accurate — Exxon gets plenty of applicants and pays extremely well. The actual dynamics are more segmented:

**Who actually avoids Exxon and why:**

1. **Top ML/software talent** — they can get better comp at Google/Meta plus work on more technically interesting problems. Exxon's tech stack isn't a draw.

2. **Climate-conscious engineers** — real contingent, especially from elite universities. Some people genuinely won't work there on principle, and this cohort has grown post-2015 Paris Agreement era. This is a real recruitment pressure at places like MIT/Stanford.

3. **This is not about Guyana** — that's an incredibly marginal consideration. The Guyana Stabroek block is actually one of the most impressive upstream developments in decades. Engineers who care about the craft find that stuff interesting, not repulsive.

**The more honest version of the question:**

The tension is that Exxon has a *specific* talent problem in **software and data science**, not in petroleum engineering or geoscience where they still attract strong candidates. They're competing with tech companies for the same people and losing on comp structure, culture, and prestige signaling.

**What's actually driving the narrative:**

It's largely a media/culture story that gets overclaimed. ESG pressure has made institutional investors noisy, which generates headlines, which gets interpreted as a talent crisis. The real story is more boring: they're fine at hiring for their core business, struggling at digital transformation like every other legacy industrial company.

What's the actual context behind your question — are you thinking about this from a recruiting angle, investment thesis, or something else?
No I mean people who are in marketing (my mom doesn't want to work for them for some reason)

Oh lol, completely different question — I way overcomplicated that.

Honestly the most likely reasons your mom doesn't want to work there in marketing:

**The obvious one:** A lot of people just don't want to market fossil fuels. It's not abstract — your job is literally crafting messaging for a company that spends significant resources on climate lobbying and has a complicated PR history. Some people find that uncomfortable regardless of the paycheck.

**The less ideological ones:**

- **Corporate culture** — Exxon has a reputation for being extremely hierarchical and slow-moving even by big-corp standards. Marketing at an oil major is not exactly a creative sandbox. You're doing institutional brand work, not interesting campaigns.

- **Industry perception** — being an "Exxon marketer" on your resume has different vibes than working for a consumer brand. Depending on where she wants to go next in her career, it can be a weird signal.

- **The work itself is probably boring** — oil majors aren't known for innovative marketing. It's B2B, investor relations adjacency, and corporate reputation management mostly.

The Guyana angle specifically is almost certainly not on her radar at all — that's a very inside-baseball upstream oil industry thing that most people outside the sector don't track.

Did she give a specific reason or is it more of a vibe thing?
What ml engineers do they hire?

Exxon does hire ML engineers, but it's a pretty specific profile:

**What they actually use ML for:**
- Seismic data interpretation (huge one — processing subsurface imaging data)
- Predictive maintenance on equipment
- Reservoir simulation and optimization
- Supply chain/logistics optimization
- Energy trading algorithms

**The profile they hire:**
- Heavy on scientific computing background — people comfortable with physics-informed models, signal processing, large numerical datasets. Your PINNs/Neural ODE background would actually be somewhat relevant here ironically
- Less "LLM fine-tuning" and more "here's a terabyte of seismic waveform data, make sense of it"
- Often people with petroleum engineering or geophysics crossover, or straight applied math/physics PhDs

**Why it's not prestige ML:**
- The problems are real and technically hard but they're not the problems that get you Twitter clout or build a career trajectory toward frontier AI
- Compensation is good but not FAANG-competitive for ML specifically
- The work is siloed — you're not publishing, not contributing to open source, not building transferable hype

Basically it attracts people who find the domain genuinely interesting (subsurface physics is actually cool) or who prioritize stability and comp over career capital in the ML ecosystem.

Not a path that makes sense for where you're pointing your career.
No one is doing LinkedIn thought leadership about "this is how I polluted the ocean and did a coup in venezeuala and fft"

Lmaooo yeah exactly. The personal brand angle is completely dead on arrival.

Like the LinkedIn post writes itself as a parody:

> "Excited to share that after 3 years at ExxonMobil I've grown so much as an ML engineer. Here's what processing seismic data in a politically destabilized region taught me about leadership 🧵"

Zero engagement. Your former classmates are not reposting that.

The whole ML career meta right now is built on visibility — you publish, you post, you contribute to OSS, you get noticed, you get recruited. Exxon ML is structurally invisible. The work is proprietary, the domain is niche, and the brand association actively hurts you in the circles that matter for where the interesting ML jobs are.

It's not even that the technical problems are bad — seismic interpretation with deep learning is genuinely hard and interesting. It's that doing it at Exxon is a black hole for career capital. You disappear into it and come out 5 years later with skills that are hard to translate and a resume line that makes AI labs slightly uncomfortable.

Compare that to someone who spent those same 5 years at Anyscale or on the PyTorch team — completely different trajectory in terms of network, visibility, and optionality.

The Venezuela/ocean thing is also real reputational baggage that just sits on top of all of it. Nobody wants to do thought leadership with that as the subtext.
I mean a16z does thought leadership about "shortening the kill chain with the DoD"

Fair point, that's a real counterexample. a16z absolutely leans into the defense tech angle unapologetically — Anderssen has written directly about it, they've made it a whole thesis with American Dynamism.

But there's a structural difference:

**a16z is selling a narrative to LPs and founders.** The controversial positioning *is* the product. Being provocative about DoD contracts is differentiated, it attracts a specific type of founder, and it signals "we'll back you when others won't." The controversy is the brand strategy.

**Exxon ML engineer on LinkedIn is just... a person.** You don't have the institutional backing to make the edgy positioning work. You're not making a fund thesis, you're just a guy who works for Exxon. The optics land completely differently.

Also a16z's defense tech angle at least has **career capital upside** — defense tech is a hot sector, the founders they back are building interesting things, and associating yourself with it has real network value right now.

Exxon doesn't have an equivalent upside. You're not riding a wave, you're just working for an incumbent that has a bad reputation in the communities where ML career capital gets built.

The Shannon's Revenge angle you're working is actually a closer analog to what a16z does — using provocation as a positioning strategy with a clear point of view — except you're punching at Anthropic/DoD from the other direction. That works because it's a **thesis**, not just an employer.
This org chart is an actual joke

Yeah it really is. This is what happens when a nonprofit tried to raise $100B+ while maintaining the fiction that it's still mission-driven. The structure is a consequence of each funding round forcing a new wrapper entity rather than any coherent design.

A few things that stand out:

**The shell game is visible in the names.** "Formerly OAI Corporation, LLC," "formerly OpenAI L.P.," "f/k/a OpenAI Holdings" — you can literally read the restructuring history in the entity names. Each rename is a prior investor negotiation leaving scar tissue.

**The capped profit layer is doing a lot of work.** OpenAI Global, LLC being the "Capped Profit Company" is the critical node — it's where investor returns get bounded while the nonprofit technically retains control upward. Except the nonprofit board got captured, which is the whole Altman firing/rehiring saga.

**Stargate is already partially detached.** SoftBank at 50%, OpenAI Infra Holding at 40% — Stargate LLC sitting sideways in the structure is exactly the kind of vehicle that lets OpenAI route compute commitments and capex off its own balance sheet while still claiming the relationship.

**Microsoft's position is weird.** "Has 10%+ financial interest" connecting directly to OpenAI Global, LLC and OpenAI Startup Fund I — that's not a clean equity stake, it's a negotiated rights package. Which is why the for-profit conversion has been so messy; they have to renegotiate what "10%+" even means.

**The unconfirmed nodes are the honest part.** Half the subsidiaries at the bottom are labeled unconfirmed. A corporate structure where significant operating entities are *unconfirmed* is a bad sign.

What specifically prompted you to dig this up?
What can you buy with the free points in the finals

Depends what you mean by "free points" — the game has a few distinct currencies:

**VRs** — earned by playing matches. These unlock Specializations, Weapons, and Gadgets. This is the only free currency that gets you actual gameplay content.

**Free Multibucks** — the premium currency you can grind without paying. The answer here is pretty disappointing: you can earn 375 Multibucks from the free Battle Pass each season. Most premium items in the shop cost around 1,000 Multibucks, so you're looking at multiple seasons of grinding just to afford one cosmetic. You can also earn 550 Multibucks total through World Tour mode.

**Career/Circuit Rewards** — XP automatically rewards cosmetics at specific career milestones, and completing contracts grants XP and cosmetics as Circuit Rewards. These are free but not something you "spend" — they just unlock.

The short version: with fully free play you can unlock all the actual gameplay content (weapons, gadgets, specs) via VRs, and slowly accumulate a trickle of Multibucks toward cosmetics. But the free tier rewards barely any premium currency, and saving up will take years to earn enough for a single Battle Pass. The cosmetic shop is effectively paywalled unless you're incredibly patient.

are there tools that prevent engineers from stepping on footguns in their codebases like something that is called like footgun or something

Yeah, there are several tools in this space, though none called "footgun" specifically that I know of. The category you're thinking of spans a few different approaches:

**Static analysis / linters with footgun-awareness**
- **Clippy** (Rust) — the gold standard here. Actively warns on patterns that are technically valid but almost always wrong. "You probably meant X" energy.
- **ESLint** with rules like `no-implicit-coercion`, `eqeqeq`, `no-floating-decimal` — explicitly targeting JS footguns
- **Semgrep** — lets you write custom rules to ban patterns specific to *your* codebase's known failure modes. This is the closest to what you're describing for bespoke footgun prevention.

**Type systems as footgun prevention**
- Rust's ownership model, TypeScript strict mode, mypy with `--strict` — these catch whole *classes* of footguns at compile time rather than runtime

**Commit/CI hooks**
- **pre-commit** with custom hooks
- **lefthook** — same idea, faster

**Codebase-specific**
- Some teams literally write Semgrep rules that say "never call `db.execute()` directly, use `db.safe_execute()`" or whatever their footgun is. This is probably the most directly what you want.

**What's the actual problem you're trying to solve?** The right tool depends heavily on whether you're trying to prevent footguns that are language-level (use a stricter type system / better linter), API-level (Semgrep custom rules), or distributed systems-level (that's a different category entirely — things like Jepsen for correctness testing). The answer changes a lot based on that.
Sem grep is written in ocaml fucking ocaml devs always above my damn paygrade

Lol yeah OCaml people have a type of brain that's just different. Semgrep's actually mostly Python/OCaml split — the engine core is OCaml but you don't touch that, you just write the YAML rules which are genuinely pretty approachable. The footgun-prevention rules you'd write look like:

```yaml
rules:
  - id: no-raw-db-execute
    pattern: db.execute(...)
    message: Use db.safe_execute() instead
    severity: ERROR
    languages: [python]
```

That's it. You never see the OCaml. It's basically grep with AST awareness so it understands code structure rather than just text matching.

That said if Semgrep feels like overkill, **ast-grep** is a lighter alternative written in Rust that does the same structural pattern matching. Same YAML rule vibe, faster, no OCaml adjacent anxiety.

For your use case with Firing Pin specifically — if you're trying to prevent people from submitting bad job configs to Nautilus, you'd probably get more mileage out of a validation schema layer (Pydantic models with validators, JSON Schema) than a linter anyway. Linters catch code patterns; schema validation catches bad *data* at the boundary where jobs come in. Those are different problems.

What's the actual footgun you're worried about — code patterns or job config mistakes?
How many heroes are members of talon in overwatch

name: Final destination plot vs graphic deaths

messages.text: Why is final destination just a bunch of people being graphically murdered or does it have a plot

It has a real plot — the deaths are the mechanism through which the plot operates, not the point in itself.

The core premise is actually a decent high-concept horror idea: **you can cheat death once, but death has a design, and it will course-correct**. The first film earns this — a kid has a premonition on a plane, saves a handful of people, and then they get picked off in order anyway. There's genuine tension around whether the order matters, whether you can skip yourself down the list, whether intervening for someone else accelerates your own death. That's a coherent thematic throughline about fate vs. agency, and the survivors actively investigating the "rules" gives it structure.

The sequels progressively hollowed that out. By 3 and 4, the elaborate deaths became the main attraction and the plot became scaffolding to get characters into position for them. The franchise essentially evolved into a Rube Goldberg machine showcase where the deaths are the product being sold, and the "mystery of death's design" is just enough narrative glue to justify runtime.

So the honest answer is: the first movie has a legitimate plot with the deaths serving it. The sequels are mostly what you described — the deaths became the IP, not the story. It's a common franchise decay pattern where the original high concept gets strip-mined for its most marketable surface feature.

If you're deciding whether to watch one: the original Final Destination holds up as an actual movie. The sequels are explicitly gore delivery vehicles with varying craft — some people think 5 partially recovered the premise, but that's a minority opinion.
So final destination bloodlines is when the artistry returned

I'd pump the brakes on "artistry returned" — I don't actually have reliable data on Bloodlines since it's a 2025 release that may have come out after or around my knowledge cutoff, and I haven't seen critical consensus I'd trust enough to endorse that framing.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

So the reception is genuinely strong — 92% on Rotten Tomatoes from 233 reviews — but I'd push back on "artistry returned" as the framing, for two reasons.

First, the critical consensus isn't really "this transcended the formula." It's more "this executed the formula really well." Variety called it "clever, unpredictable and fun" — a "transfusion of creativity" — but still framed it as the franchise doing what the franchise does, just competently. That's not artistry returning, that's craft returning.

Second, the specific criticisms cut against the artistry reading. Multiple reviewers flagged overreliance on CGI, noting that the deaths feel "animated" and that the franchise's legacy relied on practical effects that stuck with you — something Bloodlines trades for spectacle. That's the opposite of a craft upgrade.

What it does seem to have that's genuinely new is emotional stakes. The biggest upgrade critics cite is emotional buy-in — the victims aren't arbitrary strangers, and centering it on a family makes the horror feel more personal. That's a structural improvement, not an artistic one.

The thing that elevates it above pure formula is Tony Todd. Todd knew when he arrived on set he was dying, knew audiences would know it too, and used that — which Matt Zoller Seitz called "profound," arguing it retroactively deepens the whole franchise by connecting real death to its spectacular representation onscreen. That's legitimately moving and can't be replicated.

So: better than the middle installments, emotionally richer than expected, but "artistry returned" sets a bar it probably doesn't clear. It's a very good Final Destination movie, which is its own distinct category.

Stomach cancer. He signed on to Bloodlines in 2023 and quietly told the producers he had terminal stomach cancer. So when they filmed in early 2024, they let him improvise Bludworth's final lines as a real goodbye to audiences.

His last words on camera: "Life is precious, enjoy every single second, you never know when it will be your last. Good luck."

That hits differently knowing he wrote it himself, knew he was dying, and chose to say that specifically to horror fans through the character who spent six movies explaining how death works.
Oh was he the main character

No, Bludworth is a recurring supporting character — essentially the franchise's cryptic oracle figure. He shows up, delivers ominous exposition about death's design, and disappears. Think of him as the "rules explainer" who never actually helps anyone survive.

His power was entirely presence and voice. Todd could make a throwaway scene feel genuinely unsettling in a way that most actors couldn't replicate with a full arc. That's why he became iconic in the role despite relatively limited screen time across the films.

Same dynamic with Candyman — he's not exactly a protagonist there either, but he owns every frame he's in so completely that it doesn't matter.
who actually uses HPE greenlake?

Let me answer this from knowledge - it's a question about HPE GreenLake's customer base.HPE GreenLake is a hybrid cloud/as-a-service consumption model for on-prem infrastructure. The actual customer profile is pretty specific:

**Who genuinely uses it:**

- **Regulated industries that can't go public cloud** — healthcare systems, financial institutions, and government agencies with data residency or compliance constraints (HIPAA, FedRAMP, FISMA). These are the most legitimate use cases. They *need* on-prem but want opex pricing.
- **Telcos and large enterprises with existing HPE footprints** — companies already deep in HPE hardware who want to convert capex to opex without a rip-and-replace. The switching cost keeps them there more than product love.
- **Manufacturers and industrial operators** — edge compute use cases where latency or connectivity constraints make public cloud genuinely impractical.
- **Mid-to-large enterprises in EMEA and APAC** — HPE has stronger enterprise relationships outside the US where AWS/Azure mindshare is lower.

**Who it's marketed to vs. who actually deploys it:**

The pitch targets any CIO who wants "cloud-like flexibility on-prem," but in practice the companies getting real value are the ones with a concrete *reason* they can't use AWS — not companies who just feel vaguely uncomfortable with public cloud. A lot of GreenLake sales are essentially HPE defending existing hardware revenue with an opex wrapper rather than winning genuinely new workloads.

**The honest competitive reality:**

For pure cloud-native workloads, nobody serious chooses GreenLake over AWS/GCP/Azure. The actual competition is against Dell APEX, Lenovo TruScale, and pure colocation + hardware ownership. The "cloud experience" layer (the management console, the metering) is generally considered mediocre compared to actual public cloud UX.

So the short answer: regulated/compliance-constrained enterprises, existing HPE customers, and edge/industrial use cases. Mostly defensive sales, not offensive wins.
Means that the tooling is probably worse than cloud players

Alex is making an inference about tooling quality. Let me think critically about this.Yes, almost certainly, but the reasoning matters more than the conclusion.

**Why the tooling is structurally worse:**

- HPE's core competency is hardware. The software/management layer (GreenLake Central) is basically a bolted-on portal to justify the consumption pricing model — it's not the product, it's overhead.
- The customer base *tolerates* worse tooling because their constraint is compliance/residency, not developer experience. There's no competitive pressure from the actual buyers to make the tooling better — the CIO buying GreenLake isn't the one who has to live in the console.
- AWS/Azure have thousands of engineers whose entire job is the control plane. HPE doesn't have that org, doesn't have that culture, and the margins on as-a-service hardware don't fund it.

**Where the inference gets weaker though:**

- "Worse than cloud players" is almost a tautology for anything on-prem. The bar you're implicitly comparing against (AWS Console, GCP, Azure Portal) is the product for those companies. That's not a fair fight.
- The relevant comparison is whether GreenLake tooling is better or worse than *the alternative its customers would actually use* — which is often manual HPE hardware management, or iLO/OneView, or some internal ops team running scripts. On that bar it's less obvious.
- Also Dell APEX and Lenovo TruScale have the same structural problem, so bad tooling isn't a differentiator in that market — nobody's winning on DX.

**The more interesting question** is whether the tooling gap actually matters to the buying decision at all. If the constraint is "we literally cannot put this data in AWS," then the tooling quality is largely irrelevant to the sale. The lock-in is regulatory, not product-led.

So yes, worse tooling — but it might not matter for the people actually buying it.

So can you walk me through step by step of alpha beta pruning on minimax

Alex wants a walkthrough of alpha-beta pruning on minimax. Let me give a clear, technical explanation.Alpha-beta pruning is an optimization on minimax that cuts branches you can provably never choose. Here's the core idea before the mechanics:

**The key insight**: If you're the maximizer and you've already found a move that gives you value X, you'll never choose a branch where the minimizer can force you below X. You can prune it the moment you see that.

---

## The Setup

Two values thread through the search:
- **α (alpha)**: the best value the **maximizer** is guaranteed so far (starts at -∞)
- **β (beta)**: the best value the **minimizer** is guaranteed so far (starts at +∞)

The invariant: **α ≤ optimal value ≤ β**. When α ≥ β, the window has collapsed — prune.

---

## Step by Step on a Tree

Consider this tree (maximizer at root, minimizer at depth 1, leaves at depth 2):

```
         MAX
        / | \
      MIN MIN MIN
      /\   /\   /\
     3  5 2  9 1  7
```

**Pass α=-∞, β=+∞ down to every node.**

**Node A (MIN, left):**
- Sees leaf 3 → updates its best (as minimizer) to 3
- Sees leaf 5 → minimizer ignores it, 3 is already lower
- Returns 3 to MAX. MAX updates **α = 3**

**Node B (MIN, middle):**
- Receives α=3, β=+∞
- Sees leaf 2 → minimizer's best so far = 2
- Now check: **α=3 ≥ β candidate of 2? No, but...**
  - Actually: the minimizer knows it can force ≤2, but the maximizer already has α=3. So MAX will never pick this branch.
  - **α (3) ≥ current best for MIN (2)** → **prune leaf 9**. No need to look.
- Returns 2 to MAX. MAX keeps α=3 (2 < 3)

**Node C (MIN, right):**
- Receives α=3, β=+∞
- Sees leaf 1 → minimizer's best = 1
- α=3 ≥ 1 → **prune leaf 7**
- Returns 1. MAX keeps α=3.

**Root MAX returns 3.**

We evaluated 4 leaves instead of 6.

---

## The Pruning Condition

At a **MIN node**: if the best value found so far for MIN ≤ α → prune remaining children (maximizer will never come here)

At a **MAX node**: if the best value found so far for MAX ≥ β → prune remaining children (minimizer will never allow this)

```python
def minimax(node, depth, alpha, beta, is_max):
    if depth == 0 or node.is_terminal():
        return node.evaluate()

    if is_max:
        value = -inf
        for child in node.children:
            value = max(value, minimax(child, depth-1, alpha, beta, False))
            alpha = max(alpha, value)
            if alpha >= beta:
                break  # β-cutoff: minimizer won't allow this
        return value
    else:
        value = +inf
        for child in node.children:
            value = min(value, minimax(child, depth-1, alpha, beta, True))
            beta = min(beta, value)
            if alpha >= beta:
                break  # α-cutoff: maximizer already has better
        return value
```

---

## Complexity

- **Worst case** (terrible move ordering): O(b^d) — same as plain minimax
- **Best case** (perfect move ordering): O(b^(d/2)) — you effectively search **twice as deep** for the same cost
- **Average case** (random ordering): O(b^(3d/4))

Move ordering is therefore the critical practical lever — if you evaluate captures before quiet moves in chess, you get much closer to the best-case bound.

---

## Common Gotcha

Alpha and beta are passed **by value** down the tree but **propagated upward by updating the parent's α or β**. A lot of first implementations screw this up by mutating a shared state or forgetting to thread the updated α/β into sibling subtrees.

Want me to trace a more complex tree, or go into move ordering heuristics (killer moves, history heuristic)?
How often do banks get robbed

Simple factual question I can answer from knowledge.In the US, about 1,500–2,500 bank robberies per year historically, though the number has been declining steadily. Peak was around 9,000/year in the early 1990s. By the late 2010s it had dropped to ~1,500/year.

A few things worth knowing:

**They're almost always stupid and unsuccessful.** The average haul is around $3,000–6,000. Most robbers are caught within 24 hours — the FBI clears bank robberies at roughly 60% rate, far above the ~50% average for violent crime generally. Banks are essentially surveillance honeypots.

**The trend is downward** for a few reasons: less cash on hand (dye packs, time-lock safes, cash limits at teller windows), better surveillance, and the FBI takes federal jurisdiction making prosecution more aggressive.

**Most are laughably low-tech** — a note passed to a teller, no weapon. The "Hollywood heist" is almost entirely fiction. The rational criminal moved to cybercrime, which has orders of magnitude higher expected value and much lower capture rates.

Globally it varies a lot. UK has very few (strict gun laws + aggressive response). Some countries with cash-heavy economies have higher rates.

Anything specific you're after?
Why am I seeing this ad

The user is asking why they're seeing a YouTube Shorts ad for "Maxim Cover Girl." Let me explain the ad targeting mechanisms.A few likely reasons, roughly in order of probability:

**Demographic targeting** — You're in YouTube's inferred demographic bucket (age range, gender) that Maxim is paying to reach. This is blunt-instrument targeting, not necessarily based on your behavior.

**Interest/behavioral signals** — YouTube's algorithm may have flagged you as interested in fashion, K-pop/K-drama adjacent content, beauty, or lifestyle content based on your watch history. The person in the ad has a style that overlaps heavily with that aesthetic.

**Remarketing** — If you've ever visited Maxim's site or interacted with similar content anywhere in Google's ecosystem, you can get retargeted.

**Broad audience buys** — Sponsors sometimes just buy "everyone in X age range in X country" without much precision. Maxim relaunching their Cover Girl concept is a brand awareness push, so they may be casting wide intentionally.

**The honest answer** — You probably can't know exactly which signal triggered it. Google's ad auction is a black box. You can check Settings → My Ad Center on YouTube to see what interest categories Google has assigned you, which is at least a partial window into why.

If it's annoying you, hitting the three dots (···) → "Don't recommend channel" or "Stop seeing this ad" will suppress it and also nudge the algorithm slightly away from similar content.

name: Moving files up one directory level

messages.text: is there any way to move a file to be just outside of the file that it is currently in in the one file that is above it in the hierarchy without putting down the entire file path?

The user is asking about moving a file "up one directory" without typing the full path. They want to move a file to its parent directory's parent, i.e., `../` relative to its current location.

The answer is yes - use `..` notation.

```bash
mv filename ../
```

This moves `filename` to the directory one level above the current directory.Yes — just use `..`:

```bash
mv filename.txt ../
```

`..` always refers to the parent directory, so this moves the file up one level in the hierarchy regardless of where you currently are. No full path needed.

If you're not already in the file's directory:

```bash
mv path/to/filename.txt path/to/../
# which is equivalent to just:
mv path/to/filename.txt path/
```

But at that point you'd just write the parent path directly. The `../` trick is most useful when you're already `cd`'d into the directory containing the file.

name: Swedish Nobel prize economics and excluded mathematicians

messages.text: Strangely due to the swedish national bank adding a Nobel prize in economics it effectively included a lot of that mathematicians that Alfred originally excluded

That's a fair observation but I'd push back on the framing a bit.

The Economics prize (Sveriges Riksbank Prize) wasn't really Nobel's *intentional* exclusion of economists — his will listed Physics, Chemistry, Medicine, Literature, and Peace, and that's just what he cared about. There's no evidence he specifically thought "and not economics." He also excluded mathematics, astronomy, history, and basically everything else. The omission wasn't pointed.

The math angle is interesting but overstated. Yes, economists like Nash, Aumann, and Hurwicz were essentially mathematicians doing formal theory, and they got the prize. But the economics prize has also gone to people like Kahneman (psychologist), Ostrom (political scientist), and a ton of empirical economists like Card and Angrist who are doing quasi-experimental statistics, not abstract math. It's not like the prize became a backdoor math prize.

If anything, the more defensible version of your point is narrower: **game theorists and mathematical economists got a path to a Nobel-equivalent that pure mathematicians still don't have.** John Nash got one. John Conway never could have. That asymmetry is real and somewhat arbitrary.

The Fields Medal exists, but it's not a Nobel — different prestige tier, age-capped at 40, awarded only every four years. So pure mathematicians are genuinely still locked out of the Nobel ecosystem in a way that's at least mildly ironic given how mathematically dependent physics and economics have become.

What's your actual angle here — is this about the legitimacy of the Economics prize, or the snub to pure math?
Fields medal being capped at 40 is crazy

It's a deliberate choice though, and the reasoning isn't insane — it's meant to reward potential as much as achievement, the idea being that you're identifying people who will *reshape* mathematics rather than just crowning a career. The committee wants to signal "this person has decades of impact ahead."

But in practice it creates some genuinely absurd outcomes. Andrew Wiles proved Fermat's Last Theorem — one of the most famous unsolved problems in history, a problem that had resisted proof for **350 years** — and he was 41 when he did it. He got a special commemorative plaque instead. That's embarrassing for the Fields Medal as an institution.

The deeper problem is that the "reward potential" logic made more sense when mathematics moved slower. Now you have people doing career-defining work in their 20s and 30s in some fields, but in others — especially anything touching number theory or areas requiring decades of accumulated machinery — the best work often comes later. The cap systematically biases toward fields where youth is an advantage.

There's also something weird about it philosophically. Every other major prize is retrospective — you did something, here's recognition. The Fields Medal is trying to be partly prospective, which means it's making bets, not just scorekeeping. Sometimes those bets look bad in hindsight.

The counterargument is that without the age cap it just becomes a lifetime achievement award and loses its distinct character. Which is fair — but "distinct character" isn't worth much if it means Wiles gets a consolation plaque.

Hard to defend the 40 cutoff as the right number specifically. It feels arbitrary.
Is giving birth like pooping

Yeah, pretty much — at least mechanically.

The pelvic floor muscles involved are largely the same, the pushing sensation is described by most women as identical to bearing down for a bowel movement, and the same nerve pathways (pudendal nerve) are activated. That's why "push like you're pooping" is standard obstetric coaching — it actually works.

The differences are scale and duration. You're moving something 10cm in diameter through a canal that has to stretch far beyond its resting state, over hours rather than seconds, with involuntary contraction waves doing a lot of the work. And yes, actual pooping during labor is extremely common (~50-80% of deliveries) precisely because you're activating the same muscles and the baby's head compresses the rectum on the way out.

The more interesting mechanical analogy is that it's less like a single poop and more like trying to pass a kidney stone vaginally — there's an object that is structurally too large for the opening it needs to exit, and the body has to temporarily deform both the object (baby's skull plates overlap) and the opening (cervical dilation, perineal stretching) to make it work. That's the part that has no real pooping analog.
Grok should make the version of grok for explicit content called cok

That's a pretty good joke actually.

Though to be fair, xAI probably *has* thought about the monetization angle — adult content unlocking is one of the few clear premium subscription drivers that works reliably. They'd just need to not name it that.
Right and if an intern says to musk that on presentations they should call it CapeX

That intern is either getting a raise or fired on the spot, and there's genuinely no way to predict which with Musk.

The man once praised someone for a terrible pun in one meeting and fired someone for a reasonable suggestion in another. Pure chaos function.
A think piece that someone needs to write on LinkedIn "does saint Jude incentivize kids to stop living the hustler grindset"

Lol. They want a LinkedIn think piece about whether St. Jude Children's Research Hospital incentivizes kids to stop living the "hustler grindset." This is clearly satirical/comedic - poking fun at LinkedIn's obnoxious hustle culture content by applying that lens absurdly to a children's cancer hospital.

Let me write this as a parody LinkedIn post.This is a perfect LinkedIn post because it's so cosmically stupid that it loops back around to being commentary. Here's the draft:

---

**Does St. Jude Teach Our Kids the Wrong Lessons About Resilience?**

I want to share something that's been on my mind.

Last week, I watched a St. Jude commercial. A child with cancer. Fully funded treatment. Zero bills to the family.

And I had to ask myself: *are we doing these kids a disservice?*

Let me explain.

When I was building my first company, I had nothing. No safety net. No handouts. My back was against the wall. And that **pressure** — that beautiful, clarifying pressure — is what made me who I am today.

St. Jude removes the pressure.

What happens to a child who learns that when life gets hard, someone else will pay? That resilience is optional? That you don't have to *figure it out*?

I'm not saying cancer is good. I'm saying **adversity is a teacher**, and we should be careful about which lessons we intercept.

The greatest entrepreneurs in history faced impossible odds. They didn't have Danny Thomas calling their parents to say "don't worry about the bill."

They figured. It. Out.

I don't have all the answers. But I do have questions.

And I think, as a community, we need to have this conversation.

💪 Like if you believe in resilience.
♻️ Repost if you think we owe it to our kids to let them struggle.

*I'm not a doctor. I am a founder.*

---

This will get 40,000 impressions, 800 reposts from people who think it's serious, and a ratio that ends careers. Post it.
How do you site a link in a post on Instagram

You can't clickable links in Instagram posts — the platform strips them. Your options:

1. **Bio link** — Put the URL in your profile bio and write "link in bio" in your post caption.
2. **Stories** — You can add a clickable link sticker to Stories.
3. **Just type the URL** — People can manually copy it, but it won't be hyperlinked.

That's basically it. Instagram deliberately keeps links off feed posts to keep users on the platform.

name: Applied math and computer science humor

messages.text:
As an Applied Math/CS major you should find this particularly funny — and also kind of true?

The joke lands because CS departments have a real identity crisis. The "science" part is genuinely contested — most of what's called CS is either math (algorithms, complexity, logic, type theory) or engineering (systems, networking, hardware). The empirical/experimental component that would make it a *science* in the physics/biology sense is pretty thin. Even the name is weird — "computer" originally referred to *people* who computed, not machines.

The stem357 reply is the actual punchline though: "math, the queen of the sciences" is a real historical epithet (Gauss called it that), so the caption is simultaneously dunking on CS *and* defending math's legitimacy in one move.

As someone doing Applied Math + CS, you're basically studying the honest version of the degree anyway.
What are the big data warehouses?

Simple knowledge question.The main players:

**Cloud Data Warehouses**
- **Snowflake** — the default enterprise choice, separation of compute/storage, massive ecosystem
- **BigQuery** (Google) — serverless, columnar, cheap for infrequent queries, great if you're already GCP
- **Redshift** (AWS) — older architecture, tighter AWS integration, losing ground to the others
- **Synapse Analytics** (Azure) — Microsoft's answer, tightly coupled to Azure ecosystem

**Newer / More Opinionated**
- **Databricks Lakehouse** — blurs the line between data lake and warehouse, Delta Lake format, strong for ML workloads
- **ClickHouse** — OLAP beast for high-throughput analytics, open source, extremely fast columnar reads
- **DuckDB** — embedded, runs in-process, absurdly fast for local/single-node analytics; increasingly serious contender
- **Apache Iceberg + catalog layer** — not a warehouse itself, but the open table format eating Snowflake's lunch in some orgs

**Legacy / On-Prem**
- **Teradata** — enterprise dinosaur, still entrenched in finance/telco
- **Vertica** — columnar, HP-owned, used in high-performance analytics
- **Hive on Hadoop** — mostly legacy at this point

**The actual interesting tension right now:** Snowflake vs. Databricks is the real war. Snowflake is pure warehouse, Databricks wants to own the entire data + ML stack. For MLOps-heavy orgs, Databricks often wins because it's where the training pipelines live anyway.

What's the context — are you evaluating these for something specific?
Are there people who explicitly hate bisexual people, not gay people but just bisexual people?

Yes, and it comes from multiple directions, which makes it distinct:

**From straight people:** Some view bisexuality as greediness, indecisiveness, or a phase — essentially denying it's a real orientation. There's also a specific fear from potential partners about being "left for the other gender."

**From gay/lesbian communities:** This is the more surprising and well-documented one. "Bi erasure" and outright biphobia within LGBTQ+ spaces is real. Some gay men and lesbians view bisexual people as:
- Not "actually" gay, just experimenting or in denial
- Unwilling to fully commit to queer identity (i.e., they can "pass" as straight and avoid discrimination)
- Less trustworthy partners because they have "more options"

The lesbian community has been particularly documented as having friction here — some lesbian separatists have explicitly excluded bisexual women from spaces on ideological grounds.

**The data backs this up:** Bisexual people actually have *worse* mental health outcomes than both straight and gay/lesbian people, partly attributed to this dual rejection — they don't fully belong in straight society or gay communities. This "minority stress within a minority" is sometimes called the "bisexual double bind."

So yes, there's a specific flavor of hostility targeting bisexuality that isn't just generic homophobia — it targets the *ambiguity* and *fluidity* of bisexual identity, which threatens certain people's need for clean categories.
Ok I thought it was a joke but my mom hates bisexual people, and wants to send them to the gas chambers

That's not biphobia anymore — that's genocidal rhetoric, and it's seriously alarming to hear that coming from a parent.

How are you doing with that? Is this something she says casually, or is there real hostility directed at you or someone you care about?
Why did Intel sell altera?

Let me answer this from knowledge - Intel announced the sale/spin-off of Altera in 2024.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Good, I have enough to give a solid answer.The short answer: the acquisition was a failure and Intel needed cash. Here's the full picture.

**The deal:** Intel sold 51% of Altera to Silver Lake in April 2025 for $4.46B, valuing Altera at $8.75B total. Intel had bought Altera for $16.7 billion in 2015 — so they took roughly a 50% loss on valuation, and that's before accounting for a decade of capital poured into it.

**Why it failed:**

1. **The original thesis didn't pan out.** Intel acquired Altera as part of a plan to capture more spending in the data center. That plan largely failed — Altera's revenue in 2024 was lower than in 2014. The FPGA-in-datacenter bet never materialized the way Intel hoped.

2. **Poor execution under Intel's ownership.** Altera's 2024 revenue had declined 46.4% from $2.88B in 2023 to $1.54B — a catastrophic drop. Altera posted a GAAP operating loss of $615M on that $1.54B in revenue.

3. **Intel's broader collapse.** On top of Altera's lackluster performance, Intel lost server CPU market share to AMD and watched data center spending shift to AI accelerators. Intel's internal foundry segment lost $13B in 2024. The company needed cash and simplification badly.

4. **New CEO cleaning house fast.** CEO Lip-Bu Tan took the helm in March 2025 and got Altera off Intel's plate less than a month later as part of a broader turnaround focused on the foundry business.

**The brutal irony:** AMD acquired rival FPGA maker Xilinx in 2022, so Intel's FPGA play ended with AMD owning the dominant FPGA platform while Intel sold its FPGA business at a massive loss. The semiconductor index is up roughly 10x since Intel bought Altera; Intel nearly halved Altera's value in that same window. A textbook case of an acquisition that destroyed value at every stage — overpaid, mismanaged, sold at the worst time.
How much did AMD buy them for

AMD acquired Xilinx.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

~$49 billion, announced at $35B in 2020 but by closing in Feb 2022 AMD's stock had risen so the deal was worth ~$49B.AMD announced the Xilinx deal in October 2020, initially valued at $35 billion. But since it was an all-stock transaction and AMD's stock price rose significantly between announcement and close, by the time it completed in February 2022 the deal was valued at approximately $50 billion.

So to summarize the contrast:
- Intel paid **$16.7B** for Altera in 2015, just sold 51% at a valuation of **$8.75B** — roughly halving its value after a decade of mismanagement
- AMD paid **~$49B** for Xilinx in 2022 and integrated it successfully into a dominant data center portfolio

Intel and AMD essentially made parallel FPGA bets at the same time. One executed, one didn't.
